REINFORCEMENT LEARNING FOR AUTONOMOUS VEHICLES

Amit Singh¹, Suresh Pillai²
¹Department of Computer Science, Mumbai University
²Department of Data Science, IIIT Hyderabad

Abstract—
This paper presents a novel approach to reinforcement learning for autonomous vehicles using state-of-the-art machine learning
techniques. We propose a framework that addresses key challenges in Robotics by leveraging
reward architectures. Experimental results on benchmark datasets demonstrate
that our method achieves superior performance compared to existing baselines, with an improvement
of 7% in accuracy and 37% reduction in computational overhead.
Our ablation study confirms the contribution of each component.

Keywords— reward, policy, self-driving, neural networks, robotics, benchmark evaluation

I. INTRODUCTION

The rapid advancement of artificial intelligence has enabled significant breakthroughs in Robotics.
Traditional approaches suffer from limitations including high computational cost, lack of scalability,
and poor generalization to unseen data. Recent works [1,2,3] have attempted to address these challenges
using deep learning, but remain constrained by data availability and model interpretability.

In this paper, we make the following contributions:
(1) We propose a novel reward-based architecture optimized for Robotics
(2) We introduce a training strategy that reduces overfitting by 20%
(3) We conduct extensive experiments on 4 benchmark datasets
(4) We release our code and pre-trained models for reproducibility

II. RELATED WORK

Several approaches have been proposed for reinforcement learning for autonomous vehicles. Smith et al. [1] introduced a CNN-based
method achieving 79% accuracy on standard benchmarks. Lee et al. [2] proposed
an attention mechanism that improves feature extraction but requires 3x more
parameters. Wang et al. [3] applied transfer learning from ImageNet, achieving state-of-the-art
results on domain-specific tasks. Our work differs by addressing computational efficiency without
sacrificing performance.

III. METHODOLOGY

Our proposed framework consists of three main components:

A. Feature Extraction Module
We utilize a modified MobileNetV3
backbone pre-trained on ImageNet for robust feature
extraction. The backbone is fine-tuned with a learning rate of 0.001.

B. Classification Head
A 3-layer MLP with dropout (p=0.5) is attached
to the backbone output. We use Swish activations.

C. Training Objective
We optimize using AdamW with weight decay
5e-05. The loss function is cross-entropy combined with a
regularization term λ=0.001.

IV. EXPERIMENTS

Datasets: We evaluate on 4 public benchmarks.
Implementation: PyTorch 1.12, NVIDIA A100 GPU, batch size 16.

Results: Our method achieves 90.6% accuracy on the primary benchmark,
outperforming baselines by 7%. Inference time is 31ms per sample.

V. CONCLUSION

We presented a novel approach to reinforcement learning for autonomous vehicles demonstrating significant improvements over
state-of-the-art methods. Future work will explore real-time deployment.

REFERENCES
[1] A. Smith et al., "Deep Learning for Robotics," IEEE CVPR, 2021.
[2] B. Lee et al., "Attention Mechanisms in reward," NeurIPS, 2022.
[3] C. Wang et al., "Transfer Learning Survey," IEEE TPAMI, 2023.

DEEP LEARNING FOR MEDICAL IMAGE SEGMENTATION

Amit Singh¹, Rahul Verma²
¹Department of Computer Science, VIT Vellore
²Department of Data Science, Anna University

Abstract—
This paper presents a novel approach to deep learning for medical image segmentation using state-of-the-art machine learning
techniques. We propose a framework that addresses key challenges in Healthcare AI by leveraging
CNN architectures. Experimental results on benchmark datasets demonstrate
that our method achieves superior performance compared to existing baselines, with an improvement
of 4% in accuracy and 26% reduction in computational overhead.
Our ablation study confirms the contribution of each component.

Keywords— CNN, U-Net, MRI, segmentation, neural networks, healthcare ai, benchmark evaluation

I. INTRODUCTION

The rapid advancement of artificial intelligence has enabled significant breakthroughs in Healthcare AI.
Traditional approaches suffer from limitations including high computational cost, lack of scalability,
and poor generalization to unseen data. Recent works [1,2,3] have attempted to address these challenges
using deep learning, but remain constrained by data availability and model interpretability.

In this paper, we make the following contributions:
(1) We propose a novel CNN-based architecture optimized for Healthcare AI
(2) We introduce a training strategy that reduces overfitting by 34%
(3) We conduct extensive experiments on 3 benchmark datasets
(4) We release our code and pre-trained models for reproducibility

II. RELATED WORK

Several approaches have been proposed for deep learning for medical image segmentation. Smith et al. [1] introduced a CNN-based
method achieving 79% accuracy on standard benchmarks. Lee et al. [2] proposed
an attention mechanism that improves feature extraction but requires 3x more
parameters. Wang et al. [3] applied transfer learning from ImageNet, achieving state-of-the-art
results on domain-specific tasks. Our work differs by addressing computational efficiency without
sacrificing performance.

III. METHODOLOGY

Our proposed framework consists of three main components:

A. Feature Extraction Module
We utilize a modified ResNet-50
backbone pre-trained on JFT-300M for robust feature
extraction. The backbone is fine-tuned with a learning rate of 0.0001.

B. Classification Head
A 4-layer MLP with dropout (p=0.3) is attached
to the backbone output. We use GELU activations.

C. Training Objective
We optimize using AdaFactor with weight decay
0.0001. The loss function is cross-entropy combined with a
regularization term λ=0.001.

IV. EXPERIMENTS

Datasets: We evaluate on 4 public benchmarks.
Implementation: PyTorch 2.0, NVIDIA A100 GPU, batch size 32.

Results: Our method achieves 88.6% accuracy on the primary benchmark,
outperforming baselines by 6%. Inference time is 43ms per sample.

V. CONCLUSION

We presented a novel approach to deep learning for medical image segmentation demonstrating significant improvements over
state-of-the-art methods. Future work will explore multi-modal inputs.

REFERENCES
[1] A. Smith et al., "Deep Learning for Healthcare AI," IEEE CVPR, 2020.
[2] B. Lee et al., "Attention Mechanisms in CNN," NeurIPS, 2021.
[3] C. Wang et al., "Transfer Learning Survey," IEEE TPAMI, 2022.

GRAPH NEURAL NETWORKS FOR FRAUD DETECTION

Pooja Mehta¹, Amit Singh²
¹Department of Computer Science, Anna University
²Department of Data Science, Pune University

Abstract—
This paper presents a novel approach to graph neural networks for fraud detection using state-of-the-art machine learning
techniques. We propose a framework that addresses key challenges in FinTech by leveraging
GNN architectures. Experimental results on benchmark datasets demonstrate
that our method achieves superior performance compared to existing baselines, with an improvement
of 7% in accuracy and 20% reduction in computational overhead.
Our ablation study confirms the contribution of each component.

Keywords— GNN, node classification, transactions, neural networks, fintech, benchmark evaluation

I. INTRODUCTION

The rapid advancement of artificial intelligence has enabled significant breakthroughs in FinTech.
Traditional approaches suffer from limitations including high computational cost, lack of scalability,
and poor generalization to unseen data. Recent works [1,2,3] have attempted to address these challenges
using deep learning, but remain constrained by data availability and model interpretability.

In this paper, we make the following contributions:
(1) We propose a novel GNN-based architecture optimized for FinTech
(2) We introduce a training strategy that reduces overfitting by 19%
(3) We conduct extensive experiments on 4 benchmark datasets
(4) We release our code and pre-trained models for reproducibility

II. RELATED WORK

Several approaches have been proposed for graph neural networks for fraud detection. Smith et al. [1] introduced a CNN-based
method achieving 86% accuracy on standard benchmarks. Lee et al. [2] proposed
an attention mechanism that improves feature extraction but requires 6x more
parameters. Wang et al. [3] applied transfer learning from ImageNet, achieving state-of-the-art
results on domain-specific tasks. Our work differs by addressing computational efficiency without
sacrificing performance.

III. METHODOLOGY

Our proposed framework consists of three main components:

A. Feature Extraction Module
We utilize a modified MobileNetV3
backbone pre-trained on LAION-5B for robust feature
extraction. The backbone is fine-tuned with a learning rate of 0.0005.

B. Classification Head
A 2-layer MLP with dropout (p=0.5) is attached
to the backbone output. We use Swish activations.

C. Training Objective
We optimize using AdaFactor with weight decay
5e-05. The loss function is cross-entropy combined with a
regularization term λ=0.001.

IV. EXPERIMENTS

Datasets: We evaluate on 2 public benchmarks.
Implementation: PyTorch 2.1, NVIDIA A100 GPU, batch size 64.

Results: Our method achieves 95.1% accuracy on the primary benchmark,
outperforming baselines by 5%. Inference time is 31ms per sample.

V. CONCLUSION

We presented a novel approach to graph neural networks for fraud detection demonstrating significant improvements over
state-of-the-art methods. Future work will explore zero-shot generalization.

REFERENCES
[1] A. Smith et al., "Deep Learning for FinTech," IEEE CVPR, 2019.
[2] B. Lee et al., "Attention Mechanisms in GNN," NeurIPS, 2020.
[3] C. Wang et al., "Transfer Learning Survey," IEEE TPAMI, 2021.

LABORATORY REPORT

Experiment Name: Verification of Ohm's Law
Subject: Physics Lab
Student Name: Neha Agarwal
Roll Number: 208672
Batch: A1
Date of Experiment: 15/5/2024
Date of Submission: 15/5/2024

AIM / OBJECTIVE:
To verify and study verification of ohm's law and to understand the relationship between
voltage and current under controlled conditions.

THEORY / BACKGROUND:
Verification of Ohm's Law is a fundamental concept in Physics. According to the theoretical framework,
voltage is directly proportional to current
when resistance remains constant. This relationship can be expressed
mathematically and verified through controlled experimentation.

APPARATUS / MATERIALS REQUIRED:
1. Oscilloscope
2. Spring balance
3. Standard solution (0.1M)
4. Multimeter
5. LED indicators

PROCEDURE:
1. Set up the apparatus as shown in the circuit/experimental diagram.
2. Ensure all connections are secure and the circuit is properly grounded.
3. Gradually vary voltage from minimum to maximum range.
4. For each setting, record the corresponding current.
5. Repeat each reading 4 times to minimize random errors.
6. Tabulate all observations carefully.
7. Plot the graph of voltage vs current.

OBSERVATIONS:

    S.No.     voltage         current        
    ────────────────────────────────────────
    1         5.23          9.53
    2         2.29          9.06
    3         3.32          4.27
    4         2.65          9.26
    5         3.97          8.75
    6         3.92          5.39
    7         1.59          2.02
    8         5.31          8.61

CALCULATIONS:
Mean value = 7.111
Standard Deviation = 2.649
Percentage Error = 3.69%

GRAPH:
A graph of voltage vs current was plotted.
The graph shows a sinusoidal relationship,
confirming the theoretical prediction.

RESULT:
The experiment successfully verified Verification of Ohm's Law. The experimentally obtained value of
resistance = 56.059 Ω
The theoretical value = 79.451
Percentage error = 2.71%

SOURCES OF ERROR:
1. Instrumental errors due to limited precision of measuring instruments
2. Random errors due to fluctuations in voltage
3. Human parallax error during reading
4. Contact resistance in connections (for electrical experiments)

CONCLUSION:
The experiment was performed successfully. The results confirm the theoretical relationship
between voltage and current. The percentage
error of 2.06% is within acceptable limits, indicating accurate
experimental technique.

PRECAUTIONS:
1. All readings were taken after the instrument had stabilized.
2. The experiment was conducted under constant room temperature (25°C).
3. All glassware was cleaned and dried before use.

Signature of Student: _______________     Signature of Lab Instructor: _______________

text:
DATA STRUCTURES AND ALGORITHMS — LECTURE NOTES
Unit 2: Trees and Graphs
Date: 1/1/2024

============================================================
CHAPTER OVERVIEW
============================================================

This unit covers fundamental concepts of binary tree in the context of data structures and algorithms.
By the end of this unit, students should be able to:
• Explain the concept of binary tree and its significance
• Differentiate between BFS and DFS
• Apply theoretical concepts to solve problems
• Analyze real-world scenarios involving binary tree

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TOPIC 1: INTRODUCTION TO BINARY TREE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Definition:
A binary tree is defined as a mechanism for managing shared resources in a concurrent environment.

Key Properties:
1. Correctness — must satisfy all binary tree requirements
2. Reliability — performance under increasing load
3. Isolation — behavior in failure scenarios

Example:
Consider a system with 8 processes/nodes. The binary tree operates
as follows:
Step 1: Initialize the binary tree with default parameters
Step 2: Apply BFS to determine priority/order
Step 3: Execute the operation while maintaining DFS invariants
Step 4: Verify correctness using post-conditions

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TOPIC 2: BFS VS DFS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Comparison Table:
┌─────────────────┬───────────────────────┬───────────────────────┐
│ Parameter        │ BFS                   │ DFS                   │
├─────────────────┼───────────────────────┼───────────────────────┤
│ Complexity      │ O(n log n)            │ O(n²)                 │
│ Space           │ O(n)                  │ O(1)                  │
│ Use Case        │ Large datasets        │ Small datasets        │
│ Stability       │ Stable                │ Not stable            │
└─────────────────┴───────────────────────┴───────────────────────┘

Important Note: BFS is preferred when data is already partially sorted.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TOPIC 3: ALGORITHMS & COMPLEXITY ANALYSIS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Algorithm — Binary Tree:
```
function binary_tree(input):
    if input is empty:
        return null
    
    result = initialize()
    for each element in input:
        process(element)
        update(result)
    
    return result
```

Time Complexity: O(n log n)
Space Complexity: O(1)
Best Case: O(log n)
Worst Case: O(n²)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PRACTICE QUESTIONS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. [2 marks] Define binary tree and list its properties.

2. [4 marks] Compare and contrast BFS with DFS using suitable examples.

3. [6 marks] Given a scenario with 6 concurrent operations, trace the execution
   of binary tree step by step.

4. [8 marks] Design an efficient algorithm using binary tree for the following problem:
   [Problem description with constraints and expected output]

5. [Previous Year Question 2022] Explain the trade-offs between BFS and
   DFS. When would you choose one over the other? (8 marks)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
IMPORTANT FORMULAE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. Efficiency = (Useful Work Done) / (Total Resources Used)
2. Throughput = (Number of Processes Completed) / (Time Period)  
3. Waiting Time = Completion Time - Burst Time - Arrival Time
4. Turnaround Time = Completion Time - Arrival Time

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
REFERENCES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. Silberschatz, Galvin, Gagne — "Data Structures and Algorithms", 10th Edition, Wiley
2. NPTEL Video Lectures — Data Structures and Algorithms by Prof. Ravi Bhat, IIT Madras
3. GeeksForGeeks — binary tree tutorials
4. Previous year question papers — SRM University 2019

OPERATING SYSTEMS — LECTURE NOTES
Unit 3: Process Management
Date: 11/3/2024

============================================================
CHAPTER OVERVIEW
============================================================

This unit covers fundamental concepts of process in the context of operating systems.
By the end of this unit, students should be able to:
• Explain the concept of process and its significance
• Differentiate between thread and scheduling
• Apply theoretical concepts to solve problems
• Analyze real-world scenarios involving process

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TOPIC 1: INTRODUCTION TO PROCESS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Definition:
A process is defined as an abstract data structure that maintains a hierarchical relationship.

Key Properties:
1. Completeness — must satisfy all process requirements
2. Reliability — performance under increasing load
3. Atomicity — behavior in failure scenarios

Example:
Consider a system with 6 processes/nodes. The process operates
as follows:
Step 1: Initialize the process with default parameters
Step 2: Apply thread to determine priority/order
Step 3: Execute the operation while maintaining scheduling invariants
Step 4: Verify correctness using post-conditions

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TOPIC 2: THREAD VS SCHEDULING
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Comparison Table:
┌─────────────────┬───────────────────────┬───────────────────────┐
│ Parameter        │ thread                │ scheduling            │
├─────────────────┼───────────────────────┼───────────────────────┤
│ Complexity      │ O(n log n)            │ O(n²)                 │
│ Space           │ O(n)                  │ O(1)                  │
│ Use Case        │ Large datasets        │ Small datasets        │
│ Stability       │ Stable                │ Not stable            │
└─────────────────┴───────────────────────┴───────────────────────┘

Important Note: thread is preferred when stability of equal elements must be preserved.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TOPIC 3: ALGORITHMS & COMPLEXITY ANALYSIS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Algorithm — Process:
```
function process(input):
    if input is empty:
        return null
    
    result = initialize()
    for each element in input:
        process(element)
        update(result)
    
    return result
```

Time Complexity: O(log n)
Space Complexity: O(n²)
Best Case: O(log n)
Worst Case: O(n²)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PRACTICE QUESTIONS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. [2 marks] Define process and list its properties.

2. [4 marks] Compare and contrast thread with scheduling using suitable examples.

3. [6 marks] Given a scenario with 6 concurrent operations, trace the execution
   of process step by step.

4. [8 marks] Design an efficient algorithm using process for the following problem:

FEDERATED LEARNING FOR PRIVACY-PRESERVING ML

Suresh Pillai¹, Priya Patel²
¹Department of Computer Science, Mumbai University
²Department of Data Science, SRM University

Abstract—
This paper presents a novel approach to federated learning for privacy-preserving ml using state-of-the-art machine learning
techniques. We propose a framework that addresses key challenges in Distributed AI by leveraging
privacy architectures. Experimental results on benchmark datasets demonstrate
that our method achieves superior performance compared to existing baselines, with an improvement
of 5% in accuracy and 20% reduction in computational overhead.
Our ablation study confirms the contribution of each component.

Keywords— privacy, gradient, federated, neural networks, distributed ai, benchmark evaluation

I. INTRODUCTION

The rapid advancement of artificial intelligence has enabled significant breakthroughs in Distributed AI.
Traditional approaches suffer from limitations including high computational cost, lack of scalability,
and poor generalization to unseen data. Recent works [1,2,3] have attempted to address these challenges
using deep learning, but remain constrained by data availability and model interpretability.

In this paper, we make the following contributions:
(1) We propose a novel privacy-based architecture optimized for Distributed AI
(2) We introduce a training strategy that reduces overfitting by 35%
(3) We conduct extensive experiments on 3 benchmark datasets
(4) We release our code and pre-trained models for reproducibility

II. RELATED WORK

Several approaches have been proposed for federated learning for privacy-preserving ml. Smith et al. [1] introduced a CNN-based
method achieving 75% accuracy on standard benchmarks. Lee et al. [2] proposed
an attention mechanism that improves feature extraction but requires 6x more
parameters. Wang et al. [3] applied transfer learning from ImageNet, achieving state-of-the-art
results on domain-specific tasks. Our work differs by addressing computational efficiency without
sacrificing performance.

III. METHODOLOGY

Our proposed framework consists of three main components:

A. Feature Extraction Module
We utilize a modified Vision Transformer
backbone pre-trained on ImageNet for robust feature
extraction. The backbone is fine-tuned with a learning rate of 0.0001.

B. Classification Head
A 4-layer MLP with dropout (p=0.3) is attached
to the backbone output. We use GELU activations.

C. Training Objective
We optimize using AdamW with weight decay
0.0001. The loss function is cross-entropy combined with a
regularization term λ=0.1.

IV. EXPERIMENTS

Datasets: We evaluate on 3 public benchmarks.
Implementation: PyTorch 2.1, NVIDIA A100 GPU, batch size 32.

Results: Our method achieves 91.0% accuracy on the primary benchmark,
outperforming baselines by 5%. Inference time is 36ms per sample.

V. CONCLUSION

We presented a novel approach to federated learning for privacy-preserving ml demonstrating significant improvements over
state-of-the-art methods. Future work will explore multi-modal inputs.

REFERENCES
[1] A. Smith et al., "Deep Learning for Distributed AI," IEEE CVPR, 2021.
[2] B. Lee et al., "Attention Mechanisms in privacy," NeurIPS, 2022.
[3] C. Wang et al., "Transfer Learning Survey," IEEE TPAMI, 2023.

COMPUTER NETWORKS — LECTURE NOTES
Unit 5: Transport Layer
Date: 6/3/2024

============================================================
CHAPTER OVERVIEW
============================================================

This unit covers fundamental concepts of TCP in the context of computer networks.
By the end of this unit, students should be able to:
• Explain the concept of TCP and its significance
• Differentiate between UDP and socket
• Apply theoretical concepts to solve problems
• Analyze real-world scenarios involving TCP

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TOPIC 1: INTRODUCTION TO TCP
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Definition:
A TCP is defined as an abstract data structure that maintains a hierarchical relationship.

Key Properties:
1. Efficiency — must satisfy all TCP requirements
2. Consistency — performance under increasing load
3. Durability — behavior in failure scenarios

Example:
Consider a system with 5 processes/nodes. The TCP operates
as follows:
Step 1: Initialize the TCP with default parameters
Step 2: Apply UDP to determine priority/order
Step 3: Execute the operation while maintaining socket invariants
Step 4: Verify correctness using post-conditions

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TOPIC 2: UDP VS SOCKET
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Comparison Table:
┌─────────────────┬───────────────────────┬───────────────────────┐
│ Parameter        │ UDP                   │ socket                │
├─────────────────┼───────────────────────┼───────────────────────┤
│ Complexity      │ O(n log n)            │ O(n²)                 │
│ Space           │ O(n)                  │ O(1)                  │
│ Use Case        │ Large datasets        │ Small datasets        │
│ Stability       │ Stable                │ Not stable            │
└─────────────────┴───────────────────────┴───────────────────────┘

Important Note: UDP is preferred when memory is not a constraint and speed is critical.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TOPIC 3: ALGORITHMS & COMPLEXITY ANALYSIS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Algorithm — Tcp:
```
function TCP(input):
    if input is empty:
        return null
    
    result = initialize()
    for each element in input:
        process(element)
        update(result)
    
    return result
```

Time Complexity: O(log n)
Space Complexity: O(1)
Best Case: O(log n)
Worst Case: O(n)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PRACTICE QUESTIONS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. [2 marks] Define TCP and list its properties.

2. [4 marks] Compare and contrast UDP with socket using suitable examples.

3. [6 marks] Given a scenario with 6 concurrent operations, trace the execution
   of TCP step by step.

4. [8 marks] Design an efficient algorithm using TCP for the following problem:
   [Problem description with constraints and expected output]

5. [Previous Year Question 2022] Explain the trade-offs between UDP and
   socket. When would you choose one over the other? (8 marks)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
IMPORTANT FORMULAE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. Efficiency = (Useful Work Done) / (Total Resources Used)
2. Throughput = (Number of Processes Completed) / (Time Period)  
3. Waiting Time = Completion Time - Burst Time - Arrival Time
4. Turnaround Time = Completion Time - Arrival Time

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

2. NPTEL Video Lectures — Computer Networks by Prof. Arjun Sharma, IIT Delhi
3. GeeksForGeeks — TCP tutorials

TRANSFORMER ARCHITECTURE FOR CODE GENERATION

Suresh Pillai¹, Arjun Sharma²
¹Department of Computer Science, IIIT Hyderabad
²Department of Data Science, IIT Bombay

Abstract—
This paper presents a novel approach to transformer architecture for code generation using state-of-the-art machine learning
techniques. We propose a framework that addresses key challenges in NLP by leveraging
GPT architectures. Experimental results on benchmark datasets demonstrate
that our method achieves superior performance compared to existing baselines, with an improvement
of 9% in accuracy and 14% reduction in computational overhead.
Our ablation study confirms the contribution of each component.

Keywords— GPT, code synthesis, program generation, neural networks, nlp, benchmark evaluation

I. INTRODUCTION

The rapid advancement of artificial intelligence has enabled significant breakthroughs in NLP.
Traditional approaches suffer from limitations including high computational cost, lack of scalability,
and poor generalization to unseen data. Recent works [1,2,3] have attempted to address these challenges
using deep learning, but remain constrained by data availability and model interpretability.

In this paper, we make the following contributions:
(1) We propose a novel GPT-based architecture optimized for NLP
(2) We introduce a training strategy that reduces overfitting by 16%
(3) We conduct extensive experiments on 3 benchmark datasets
(4) We release our code and pre-trained models for reproducibility

II. RELATED WORK

Several approaches have been proposed for transformer architecture for code generation. Smith et al. [1] introduced a CNN-based
method achieving 78% accuracy on standard benchmarks. Lee et al. [2] proposed
an attention mechanism that improves feature extraction but requires 5x more
parameters. Wang et al. [3] applied transfer learning from ImageNet, achieving state-of-the-art
results on domain-specific tasks. Our work differs by addressing computational efficiency without
sacrificing performance.

III. METHODOLOGY

Our proposed framework consists of three main components:

A. Feature Extraction Module
We utilize a modified EfficientNet-B4
backbone pre-trained on JFT-300M for robust feature
extraction. The backbone is fine-tuned with a learning rate of 0.001.

B. Classification Head
A 3-layer MLP with dropout (p=0.3) is attached
to the backbone output. We use ReLU activations.

C. Training Objective
We optimize using AdaFactor with weight decay
1e-05. The loss function is cross-entropy combined with a
regularization term λ=0.01.

IV. EXPERIMENTS

Datasets: We evaluate on 4 public benchmarks.
Implementation: PyTorch 1.12, NVIDIA A100 GPU, batch size 32.

Results: Our method achieves 95.1% accuracy on the primary benchmark,
outperforming baselines by 2%. Inference time is 47ms per sample.

V. CONCLUSION

We presented a novel approach to transformer architecture for code generation demonstrating significant improvements over

DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS — LECTURE NOTES
Unit 4: SQL and Transactions
Date: 23/10/2024

============================================================
CHAPTER OVERVIEW
============================================================

This unit covers fundamental concepts of SQL in the context of database management systems.
By the end of this unit, students should be able to:
• Explain the concept of SQL and its significance
• Differentiate between ACID and transaction
• Apply theoretical concepts to solve problems
• Analyze real-world scenarios involving SQL

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TOPIC 1: INTRODUCTION TO SQL
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Definition:
A SQL is defined as a mechanism for managing shared resources in a concurrent environment.

Key Properties:
1. Correctness — must satisfy all SQL requirements
2. Consistency — performance under increasing load
3. Atomicity — behavior in failure scenarios

Example:
Consider a system with 4 processes/nodes. The SQL operates
as follows:
Step 1: Initialize the SQL with default parameters
Step 2: Apply ACID to determine priority/order
Step 3: Execute the operation while maintaining transaction invariants
Step 4: Verify correctness using post-conditions

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TOPIC 2: ACID VS TRANSACTION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Comparison Table:
┌─────────────────┬───────────────────────┬───────────────────────┐
│ Parameter        │ ACID                  │ transaction           │
├─────────────────┼───────────────────────┼───────────────────────┤
│ Complexity      │ O(n log n)            │ O(n²)                 │
│ Space           │ O(n)                  │ O(1)                  │
│ Use Case        │ Large datasets        │ Small datasets        │
│ Stability       │ Stable                │ Not stable            │
└─────────────────┴───────────────────────┴───────────────────────┘

Important Note: ACID is preferred when data is already partially sorted.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TOPIC 3: ALGORITHMS & COMPLEXITY ANALYSIS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Algorithm — Sql:
```
function SQL(input):
    if input is empty:
        return null
    
    result = initialize()
    for each element in input:
        process(element)
        update(result)
    
    return result
```

Time Complexity: O(log n)
Space Complexity: O(n²)
Best Case: O(n)
Worst Case: O(n)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PRACTICE QUESTIONS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. [2 marks] Define SQL and list its properties.

2. [4 marks] Compare and contrast ACID with transaction using suitable examples.

3. [6 marks] Given a scenario with 4 concurrent operations, trace the execution
   of SQL step by step.

4. [8 marks] Design an efficient algorithm using SQL for the following problem:
   [Problem description with constraints and expected output]

5. [Previous Year Question 2022] Explain the trade-offs between ACID and
   transaction. When would you choose one over the other? (8 marks)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
IMPORTANT FORMULAE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. Efficiency = (Useful Work Done) / (Total Resources Used)
2. Throughput = (Number of Processes Completed) / (Time Period)  
3. Waiting Time = Completion Time - Burst Time - Arrival Time
4. Turnaround Time = Completion Time - Arrival Time

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
REFERENCES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. Silberschatz, Galvin, Gagne — "Database Management Systems", 10th Edition, Wiley
2. NPTEL Video Lectures — Database Management Systems by Prof. Karthik Reddy, IIT Delhi
3. GeeksForGeeks — SQL tutorials
4. Previous year question papers — Pune University 2018

TRANSFORMER ARCHITECTURE FOR CODE GENERATION

Suresh Pillai¹, Neha Agarwal²
¹Department of Computer Science, NIT Trichy
²Department of Data Science, IIT Delhi

Abstract—
This paper presents a novel approach to transformer architecture for code generation using state-of-the-art machine learning
techniques. We propose a framework that addresses key challenges in NLP by leveraging
GPT architectures. Experimental results on benchmark datasets demonstrate
that our method achieves superior performance compared to existing baselines, with an improvement
of 11% in accuracy and 28% reduction in computational overhead.
Our ablation study confirms the contribution of each component.

Keywords— GPT, code synthesis, program generation, neural networks, nlp, benchmark evaluation

I. INTRODUCTION

The rapid advancement of artificial intelligence has enabled significant breakthroughs in NLP.
Traditional approaches suffer from limitations including high computational cost, lack of scalability,
and poor generalization to unseen data. Recent works [1,2,3] have attempted to address these challenges
using deep learning, but remain constrained by data availability and model interpretability.

In this paper, we make the following contributions:
(1) We propose a novel GPT-based architecture optimized for NLP
(2) We introduce a training strategy that reduces overfitting by 15%
(3) We conduct extensive experiments on 4 benchmark datasets
(4) We release our code and pre-trained models for reproducibility

II. RELATED WORK

Several approaches have been proposed for transformer architecture for code generation. Smith et al. [1] introduced a CNN-based
method achieving 82% accuracy on standard benchmarks. Lee et al. [2] proposed
an attention mechanism that improves feature extraction but requires 5x more
parameters. Wang et al. [3] applied transfer learning from ImageNet, achieving state-of-the-art
results on domain-specific tasks. Our work differs by addressing computational efficiency without
sacrificing performance.

III. METHODOLOGY

Our proposed framework consists of three main components:

A. Feature Extraction Module
We utilize a modified EfficientNet-B4
backbone pre-trained on LAION-5B for robust feature
extraction. The backbone is fine-tuned with a learning rate of 0.001.

B. Classification Head
A 2-layer MLP with dropout (p=0.4) is attached
to the backbone output. We use Swish activations.

C. Training Objective
We optimize using AdaFactor with weight decay
1e-05. The loss function is cross-entropy combined with a
regularization term λ=0.01.

IV. EXPERIMENTS

Datasets: We evaluate on 2 public benchmarks.
Implementation: PyTorch 1.12, NVIDIA A100 GPU, batch size 16.

Results: Our method achieves 94.5% accuracy on the primary benchmark,
outperforming baselines by 6%. Inference time is 36ms per sample.

V. CONCLUSION

We presented a novel approach to transformer architecture for code generation demonstrating significant improvements over
state-of-the-art methods. Future work will explore real-time deployment.

REFERENCES
[1] A. Smith et al., "Deep Learning for NLP," IEEE CVPR, 2020.
[2] B. Lee et al., "Attention Mechanisms in GPT," NeurIPS, 2021.
[3] C. Wang et al., "Transfer Learning Survey," IEEE TPAMI, 2022.

DATA STRUCTURES AND ALGORITHMS — LECTURE NOTES
Unit 2: Trees and Graphs
Date: 4/9/2024

============================================================
CHAPTER OVERVIEW
============================================================

This unit covers fundamental concepts of binary tree in the context of data structures and algorithms.
By the end of this unit, students should be able to:
• Explain the concept of binary tree and its significance
• Differentiate between BFS and DFS
• Apply theoretical concepts to solve problems
• Analyze real-world scenarios involving binary tree

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TOPIC 1: INTRODUCTION TO BINARY TREE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Definition:
A binary tree is defined as an abstract data structure that maintains a hierarchical relationship.

Key Properties:
1. Completeness — must satisfy all binary tree requirements
2. Reliability — performance under increasing load
3. Isolation — behavior in failure scenarios

Example:
Consider a system with 7 processes/nodes. The binary tree operates
as follows:
Step 1: Initialize the binary tree with default parameters
Step 2: Apply BFS to determine priority/order
Step 3: Execute the operation while maintaining DFS invariants
Step 4: Verify correctness using post-conditions

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TOPIC 2: BFS VS DFS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Comparison Table:
┌─────────────────┬───────────────────────┬───────────────────────┐
│ Parameter        │ BFS                   │ DFS                   │
├─────────────────┼───────────────────────┼───────────────────────┤
│ Complexity      │ O(n log n)            │ O(n²)                 │
│ Space           │ O(n)                  │ O(1)                  │
│ Use Case        │ Large datasets        │ Small datasets        │
│ Stability       │ Stable                │ Not stable            │
└─────────────────┴───────────────────────┴───────────────────────┘

Important Note: BFS is preferred when memory is not a constraint and speed is critical.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TOPIC 3: ALGORITHMS & COMPLEXITY ANALYSIS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Algorithm — Binary Tree:
```
function binary_tree(input):
    if input is empty:
        return null
    
    result = initialize()
    for each element in input:
        process(element)
        update(result)
    
    return result
```

Time Complexity: O(log n)
Space Complexity: O(log n)
Best Case: O(log n)
Worst Case: O(n²)

text:
FEDERATED LEARNING FOR PRIVACY-PRESERVING ML

Meera Joshi¹, Rohan Gupta²
¹Department of Computer Science, Amity University
²Department of Data Science, IIT Delhi

Abstract—
This paper presents a novel approach to federated learning for privacy-preserving ml using state-of-the-art machine learning
techniques. We propose a framework that addresses key challenges in Distributed AI by leveraging
privacy architectures. Experimental results on benchmark datasets demonstrate
that our method achieves superior performance compared to existing baselines, with an improvement
of 7% in accuracy and 29% reduction in computational overhead.
Our ablation study confirms the contribution of each component.

Keywords— privacy, gradient, federated, neural networks, distributed ai, benchmark evaluation

I. INTRODUCTION

The rapid advancement of artificial intelligence has enabled significant breakthroughs in Distributed AI.
Traditional approaches suffer from limitations including high computational cost, lack of scalability,
and poor generalization to unseen data. Recent works [1,2,3] have attempted to address these challenges
using deep learning, but remain constrained by data availability and model interpretability.

In this paper, we make the following contributions:
(1) We propose a novel privacy-based architecture optimized for Distributed AI
(2) We introduce a training strategy that reduces overfitting by 21%
(3) We conduct extensive experiments on 4 benchmark datasets
(4) We release our code and pre-trained models for reproducibility

II. RELATED WORK

Several approaches have been proposed for federated learning for privacy-preserving ml. Smith et al. [1] introduced a CNN-based
method achieving 76% accuracy on standard benchmarks. Lee et al. [2] proposed
an attention mechanism that improves feature extraction but requires 6x more
parameters. Wang et al. [3] applied transfer learning from ImageNet, achieving state-of-the-art
results on domain-specific tasks. Our work differs by addressing computational efficiency without
sacrificing performance.

III. METHODOLOGY

Our proposed framework consists of three main components:

A. Feature Extraction Module
We utilize a modified EfficientNet-B4
backbone pre-trained on JFT-300M for robust feature
extraction. The backbone is fine-tuned with a learning rate of 0.0001.

B. Classification Head
A 4-layer MLP with dropout (p=0.4) is attached
to the backbone output. We use ReLU activations.

C. Training Objective
We optimize using SGD with Momentum with weight decay
0.0001. The loss function is cross-entropy combined with a
regularization term λ=0.1.

IV. EXPERIMENTS

Datasets: We evaluate on 4 public benchmarks.
Implementation: PyTorch 2.1, NVIDIA A100 GPU, batch size 16.

Results: Our method achieves 94.7% accuracy on the primary benchmark,
outperforming baselines by 5%. Inference time is 41ms per sample.

V. CONCLUSION

We presented a novel approach to federated learning for privacy-preserving ml demonstrating significant improvements over
state-of-the-art methods. Future work will explore zero-shot generalization.

REFERENCES
[1] A. Smith et al., "Deep Learning for Distributed AI," IEEE CVPR, 2022.
[2] B. Lee et al., "Attention Mechanisms in privacy," NeurIPS, 2023.
[3] C. Wang et al., "Transfer Learning Survey," IEEE TPAMI, 2024.

TRANSFORMER ARCHITECTURE FOR CODE GENERATION

Priya Patel¹, Neha Agarwal²
¹Department of Computer Science, Mumbai University
²Department of Data Science, NIT Trichy

Abstract—
This paper presents a novel approach to transformer architecture for code generation using state-of-the-art machine learning
techniques. We propose a framework that addresses key challenges in NLP by leveraging
GPT architectures. Experimental results on benchmark datasets demonstrate
that our method achieves superior performance compared to existing baselines, with an improvement
of 10% in accuracy and 12% reduction in computational overhead.
Our ablation study confirms the contribution of each component.

Keywords— GPT, code synthesis, program generation, neural networks, nlp, benchmark evaluation

I. INTRODUCTION

The rapid advancement of artificial intelligence has enabled significant breakthroughs in NLP.
Traditional approaches suffer from limitations including high computational cost, lack of scalability,
and poor generalization to unseen data. Recent works [1,2,3] have attempted to address these challenges
using deep learning, but remain constrained by data availability and model interpretability.

In this paper, we make the following contributions:
(1) We propose a novel GPT-based architecture optimized for NLP
(2) We introduce a training strategy that reduces overfitting by 16%
(3) We conduct extensive experiments on 5 benchmark datasets
(4) We release our code and pre-trained models for reproducibility

II. RELATED WORK

Several approaches have been proposed for transformer architecture for code generation. Smith et al. [1] introduced a CNN-based
method achieving 75% accuracy on standard benchmarks. Lee et al. [2] proposed
an attention mechanism that improves feature extraction but requires 5x more
parameters. Wang et al. [3] applied transfer learning from ImageNet, achieving state-of-the-art
results on domain-specific tasks. Our work differs by addressing computational efficiency without
sacrificing performance.

III. METHODOLOGY

Our proposed framework consists of three main components:

A. Feature Extraction Module
We utilize a modified Vision Transformer
backbone pre-trained on ImageNet for robust feature
extraction. The backbone is fine-tuned with a learning rate of 0.0001.

B. Classification Head
A 3-layer MLP with dropout (p=0.3) is attached
to the backbone output. We use GELU activations.

C. Training Objective
We optimize using AdamW with weight decay
5e-05. The loss function is cross-entropy combined with a
regularization term λ=0.01.

IV. EXPERIMENTS

Datasets: We evaluate on 4 public benchmarks.
Implementation: PyTorch 1.12, NVIDIA A100 GPU, batch size 32.

Results: Our method achieves 92.9% accuracy on the primary benchmark,
outperforming baselines by 6%. Inference time is 42ms per sample.

V. CONCLUSION

We presented a novel approach to transformer architecture for code generation demonstrating significant improvements over
state-of-the-art methods. Future work will explore zero-shot generalization.

REFERENCES
[1] A. Smith et al., "Deep Learning for NLP," IEEE CVPR, 2020.
[2] B. Lee et al., "Attention Mechanisms in GPT," NeurIPS, 2021.
[3] C. Wang et al., "Transfer Learning Survey," IEEE TPAMI, 2022.

TRANSFORMER ARCHITECTURE FOR CODE GENERATION

Ananya Das¹, Sneha Iyer²
¹Department of Computer Science, IIT Bombay
²Department of Data Science, SRM University

Abstract—
This paper presents a novel approach to transformer architecture for code generation using state-of-the-art machine learning
techniques. We propose a framework that addresses key challenges in NLP by leveraging
GPT architectures. Experimental results on benchmark datasets demonstrate
that our method achieves superior performance compared to existing baselines, with an improvement
of 4% in accuracy and 39% reduction in computational overhead.
Our ablation study confirms the contribution of each component.

Keywords— GPT, code synthesis, program generation, neural networks, nlp, benchmark evaluation

I. INTRODUCTION

The rapid advancement of artificial intelligence has enabled significant breakthroughs in NLP.
Traditional approaches suffer from limitations including high computational cost, lack of scalability,
and poor generalization to unseen data. Recent works [1,2,3] have attempted to address these challenges
using deep learning, but remain constrained by data availability and model interpretability.

In this paper, we make the following contributions:
(1) We propose a novel GPT-based architecture optimized for NLP
(2) We introduce a training strategy that reduces overfitting by 18%
(3) We conduct extensive experiments on 6 benchmark datasets
(4) We release our code and pre-trained models for reproducibility

II. RELATED WORK

Several approaches have been proposed for transformer architecture for code generation. Smith et al. [1] introduced a CNN-based
method achieving 77% accuracy on standard benchmarks. Lee et al. [2] proposed
an attention mechanism that improves feature extraction but requires 3x more
parameters. Wang et al. [3] applied transfer learning from ImageNet, achieving state-of-the-art
results on domain-specific tasks. Our work differs by addressing computational efficiency without
sacrificing performance.

III. METHODOLOGY

Our proposed framework consists of three main components:

A. Feature Extraction Module
We utilize a modified ResNet-50
backbone pre-trained on LAION-5B for robust feature
extraction. The backbone is fine-tuned with a learning rate of 0.001.

B. Classification Head
A 2-layer MLP with dropout (p=0.4) is attached
to the backbone output. We use Swish activations.

C. Training Objective
We optimize using AdamW with weight decay
1e-05. The loss function is cross-entropy combined with a
regularization term λ=0.001.

IV. EXPERIMENTS

Datasets: We evaluate on 3 public benchmarks.
Implementation: PyTorch 2.0, NVIDIA A100 GPU, batch size 16.

Results: Our method achieves 88.0% accuracy on the primary benchmark,
outperforming baselines by 3%. Inference time is 14ms per sample.

V. CONCLUSION

We presented a novel approach to transformer architecture for code generation demonstrating significant improvements over
state-of-the-art methods. Future work will explore real-time deployment.

REFERENCES
[1] A. Smith et al., "Deep Learning for NLP," IEEE CVPR, 2019.
[2] B. Lee et al., "Attention Mechanisms in GPT," NeurIPS, 2020.
[3] C. Wang et al., "Transfer Learning Survey," IEEE TPAMI, 2021.

Experiment Name: Enzyme Kinetics — Effect of Temperature on Amylase
Subject: Biology Lab
Student Name: Vikram Nair

Date of Submission: 25/9/2024

AIM / OBJECTIVE:
To verify and study enzyme kinetics — effect of temperature on amylase and to understand the relationship between
enzyme and substrate under controlled conditions.

THEORY / BACKGROUND:
Enzyme Kinetics — Effect of Temperature on Amylase is a fundamental concept in Biology. According to the theoretical framework,
enzyme is directly proportional to substrate
when rate remains constant. This relationship can be expressed
mathematically and verified through controlled experimentation.

APPARATUS / MATERIALS REQUIRED:
1. Oscilloscope
2. Breadboard
3. Masses
4. Stopwatch
5. Pendulum bob

PROCEDURE:
1. Set up the apparatus as shown in the circuit/experimental diagram.
2. Ensure all connections are secure and the circuit is properly grounded.
3. Gradually vary enzyme from minimum to maximum range.
4. For each setting, record the corresponding substrate.
5. Repeat each reading 3 times to minimize random errors.
6. Tabulate all observations carefully.
7. Plot the graph of enzyme vs substrate.

OBSERVATIONS:

    S.No.     enzyme          substrate      
    ────────────────────────────────────────
    1         4.42          7.35
    2         5.27          2.31
    3         8.83          1.11
    4         9.35          5.04
    5         7.11          9.66
    6         7.15          1.37

CALCULATIONS:
Mean value = 4.473
Standard Deviation = 3.192
Percentage Error = 3.71%

GRAPH:
A graph of enzyme vs substrate was plotted.
The graph shows a sinusoidal relationship,
confirming the theoretical prediction.

RESULT:
The experiment successfully verified Enzyme Kinetics — Effect of Temperature on Amylase. The experimentally obtained value of
rate = 65.78 Hz
The theoretical value = 46.65
Percentage error = 3.41%

SOURCES OF ERROR:
1. Instrumental errors due to limited precision of measuring instruments
2. Random errors due to fluctuations in enzyme
3. Human parallax error during reading
4. Contact resistance in connections (for electrical experiments)

CONCLUSION:
The experiment was performed successfully. The results confirm the theoretical relationship
between enzyme and substrate. The percentage
error of 1.28% is within acceptable limits, indicating accurate
experimental technique.

PRECAUTIONS:
1. All readings were taken after the instrument had stabilized.
2. The experiment was conducted under constant room temperature (27°C).
3. All glassware was cleaned and dried before use.

DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS — LECTURE NOTES
Unit 4: SQL and Transactions
Date: 10/12/2024

============================================================
CHAPTER OVERVIEW
============================================================

This unit covers fundamental concepts of SQL in the context of database management systems.
By the end of this unit, students should be able to:
• Explain the concept of SQL and its significance
• Differentiate between ACID and transaction
• Apply theoretical concepts to solve problems
• Analyze real-world scenarios involving SQL

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TOPIC 1: INTRODUCTION TO SQL
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Definition:
A SQL is defined as a protocol that ensures reliable data transmission over unreliable channels.

Key Properties:
1. Efficiency — must satisfy all SQL requirements
2. Reliability — performance under increasing load
3. Isolation — behavior in failure scenarios

Example:
Consider a system with 7 processes/nodes. The SQL operates
as follows:
Step 1: Initialize the SQL with default parameters
Step 2: Apply ACID to determine priority/order
Step 3: Execute the operation while maintaining transaction invariants
Step 4: Verify correctness using post-conditions

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TOPIC 2: ACID VS TRANSACTION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Comparison Table:
┌─────────────────┬───────────────────────┬───────────────────────┐
│ Parameter        │ ACID                  │ transaction           │
├─────────────────┼───────────────────────┼───────────────────────┤
│ Complexity      │ O(n log n)            │ O(n²)                 │
│ Space           │ O(n)                  │ O(1)                  │
│ Use Case        │ Large datasets        │ Small datasets        │
│ Stability       │ Stable                │ Not stable            │
└─────────────────┴───────────────────────┴───────────────────────┘

Important Note: ACID is preferred when data is already partially sorted.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TOPIC 3: ALGORITHMS & COMPLEXITY ANALYSIS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Algorithm — Sql:
```
function SQL(input):
    if input is empty:
        return null
    
    result = initialize()
    for each element in input:
        process(element)
        update(result)
    
    return result
```

Time Complexity: O(n²)
Space Complexity: O(1)
Best Case: O(n)
Worst Case: O(n)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PRACTICE QUESTIONS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. [2 marks] Define SQL and list its properties.

2. [4 marks] Compare and contrast ACID with transaction using suitable examples.

3. [6 marks] Given a scenario with 3 concurrent operations, trace the execution
   of SQL step by step.

4. [8 marks] Design an efficient algorithm using SQL for the following problem:
   [Problem description with constraints and expected output]

5. [Previous Year Question 2022] Explain the trade-offs between ACID and
   transaction. When would you choose one over the other? (8 marks)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
IMPORTANT FORMULAE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. Efficiency = (Useful Work Done) / (Total Resources Used)
2. Throughput = (Number of Processes Completed) / (Time Period)  
3. Waiting Time = Completion Time - Burst Time - Arrival Time
4. Turnaround Time = Completion Time - Arrival Time

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
REFERENCES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. Silberschatz, Galvin, Gagne — "Database Management Systems", 10th Edition, Wiley
2. NPTEL Video Lectures — Database Management Systems by Prof. Sneha Iyer, IIT Delhi
3. GeeksForGeeks — SQL tutorials
4. Previous year question papers — Amity University 2019

GRAPH NEURAL NETWORKS FOR FRAUD DETECTION

Ananya Das¹, Vikram Nair²
¹Department of Computer Science, Manipal Institute of Technology
²Department of Data Science, Mumbai University

Abstract—
This paper presents a novel approach to graph neural networks for fraud detection using state-of-the-art machine learning
techniques. We propose a framework that addresses key challenges in FinTech by leveraging
GNN architectures. Experimental results on benchmark datasets demonstrate
that our method achieves superior performance compared to existing baselines, with an improvement
of 4% in accuracy and 25% reduction in computational overhead.
Our ablation study confirms the contribution of each component.

Keywords— GNN, node classification, transactions, neural networks, fintech, benchmark evaluation

I. INTRODUCTION

The rapid advancement of artificial intelligence has enabled significant breakthroughs in FinTech.
Traditional approaches suffer from limitations including high computational cost, lack of scalability,
and poor generalization to unseen data. Recent works [1,2,3] have attempted to address these challenges
using deep learning, but remain constrained by data availability and model interpretability.

In this paper, we make the following contributions:
(1) We propose a novel GNN-based architecture optimized for FinTech
(2) We introduce a training strategy that reduces overfitting by 16%
(3) We conduct extensive experiments on 3 benchmark datasets
(4) We release our code and pre-trained models for reproducibility

II. RELATED WORK

Several approaches have been proposed for graph neural networks for fraud detection. Smith et al. [1] introduced a CNN-based
method achieving 82% accuracy on standard benchmarks. Lee et al. [2] proposed
an attention mechanism that improves feature extraction but requires 4x more
parameters. Wang et al. [3] applied transfer learning from ImageNet, achieving state-of-the-art
results on domain-specific tasks. Our work differs by addressing computational efficiency without
sacrificing performance.

III. METHODOLOGY

Our proposed framework consists of three main components:

A. Feature Extraction Module
We utilize a modified ResNet-50
backbone pre-trained on LAION-5B for robust feature
extraction. The backbone is fine-tuned with a learning rate of 0.0005.

B. Classification Head
A 4-layer MLP with dropout (p=0.4) is attached
to the backbone output. We use GELU activations.

C. Training Objective
We optimize using AdamW with weight decay
0.0001. The loss function is cross-entropy combined with a
regularization term λ=0.01.

IV. EXPERIMENTS

Datasets: We evaluate on 2 public benchmarks.
Implementation: PyTorch 2.0, NVIDIA A100 GPU, batch size 32.

Results: Our method achieves 96.9% accuracy on the primary benchmark,
outperforming baselines by 5%. Inference time is 21ms per sample.

V. CONCLUSION

We presented a novel approach to graph neural networks for fraud detection demonstrating significant improvements over
state-of-the-art methods. Future work will explore real-time deployment.

REFERENCES
[1] A. Smith et al., "Deep Learning for FinTech," IEEE CVPR, 2021.
[2] B. Lee et al., "Attention Mechanisms in GNN," NeurIPS, 2022.
[3] C. Wang et al., "Transfer Learning Survey," IEEE TPAMI, 2023.

text:
MACHINE LEARNING — LECTURE NOTES
Unit 6: Neural Networks
Date: 3/3/2024

============================================================
CHAPTER OVERVIEW
============================================================

This unit covers fundamental concepts of perceptron in the context of machine learning.
By the end of this unit, students should be able to:
• Explain the concept of perceptron and its significance
• Differentiate between backpropagation and gradient descent
• Apply theoretical concepts to solve problems
• Analyze real-world scenarios involving perceptron

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TOPIC 1: INTRODUCTION TO PERCEPTRON
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Definition:
A perceptron is defined as a mechanism for managing shared resources in a concurrent environment.

Key Properties:
1. Correctness — must satisfy all perceptron requirements
2. Reliability — performance under increasing load
3. Isolation — behavior in failure scenarios

Example:
Consider a system with 5 processes/nodes. The perceptron operates
as follows:
Step 1: Initialize the perceptron with default parameters
Step 2: Apply backpropagation to determine priority/order
Step 3: Execute the operation while maintaining gradient descent invariants
Step 4: Verify correctness using post-conditions

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TOPIC 2: BACKPROPAGATION VS GRADIENT DESCENT
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Comparison Table:
┌─────────────────┬───────────────────────┬───────────────────────┐
│ Parameter        │ backpropagation       │ gradient descent      │
├─────────────────┼───────────────────────┼───────────────────────┤
│ Complexity      │ O(n log n)            │ O(n²)                 │
│ Space           │ O(n)                  │ O(1)                  │
│ Use Case        │ Large datasets        │ Small datasets        │
│ Stability       │ Stable                │ Not stable            │
└─────────────────┴───────────────────────┴───────────────────────┘

Important Note: backpropagation is preferred when memory is not a constraint and speed is critical.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TOPIC 3: ALGORITHMS & COMPLEXITY ANALYSIS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Algorithm — Perceptron:
```
function perceptron(input):
    if input is empty:
        return null
    
    result = initialize()
    for each element in input:
        process(element)
        update(result)
    
    return result
```

Time Complexity: O(1)
Space Complexity: O(n²)
Best Case: O(n)
Worst Case: O(n²)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PRACTICE QUESTIONS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. [2 marks] Define perceptron and list its properties.

2. [4 marks] Compare and contrast backpropagation with gradient descent using suitable examples.

3. [6 marks] Given a scenario with 6 concurrent operations, trace the execution
   of perceptron step by step.

4. [8 marks] Design an efficient algorithm using perceptron for the following problem:
   [Problem description with constraints and expected output]

5. [Previous Year Question 2022] Explain the trade-offs between backpropagation and
   gradient descent. When would you choose one over the other? (8 marks)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
IMPORTANT FORMULAE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. Efficiency = (Useful Work Done) / (Total Resources Used)
2. Throughput = (Number of Processes Completed) / (Time Period)  
3. Waiting Time = Completion Time - Burst Time - Arrival Time
4. Turnaround Time = Completion Time - Arrival Time

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
REFERENCES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. Silberschatz, Galvin, Gagne — "Machine Learning", 10th Edition, Wiley
2. NPTEL Video Lectures — Machine Learning by Prof. Amit Singh, IIT Bombay
3. GeeksForGeeks — perceptron tutorials
4. Previous year question papers — Anna University 2019

TRANSFORMER ARCHITECTURE FOR CODE GENERATION

Ananya Das¹, Ananya Das²
¹Department of Computer Science, IIT Bombay
²Department of Data Science, Mumbai University

Abstract—
This paper presents a novel approach to transformer architecture for code generation using state-of-the-art machine learning
techniques. We propose a framework that addresses key challenges in NLP by leveraging
GPT architectures. Experimental results on benchmark datasets demonstrate
that our method achieves superior performance compared to existing baselines, with an improvement
of 8% in accuracy and 27% reduction in computational overhead.
Our ablation study confirms the contribution of each component.

Keywords— GPT, code synthesis, program generation, neural networks, nlp, benchmark evaluation

I. INTRODUCTION

The rapid advancement of artificial intelligence has enabled significant breakthroughs in NLP.
Traditional approaches suffer from limitations including high computational cost, lack of scalability,
and poor generalization to unseen data. Recent works [1,2,3] have attempted to address these challenges
using deep learning, but remain constrained by data availability and model interpretability.

In this paper, we make the following contributions:
(1) We propose a novel GPT-based architecture optimized for NLP
(2) We introduce a training strategy that reduces overfitting by 34%
(3) We conduct extensive experiments on 6 benchmark datasets
(4) We release our code and pre-trained models for reproducibility

II. RELATED WORK

Several approaches have been proposed for transformer architecture for code generation. Smith et al. [1] introduced a CNN-based
method achieving 86% accuracy on standard benchmarks. Lee et al. [2] proposed
an attention mechanism that improves feature extraction but requires 8x more
parameters. Wang et al. [3] applied transfer learning from ImageNet, achieving state-of-the-art
results on domain-specific tasks. Our work differs by addressing computational efficiency without
sacrificing performance.

III. METHODOLOGY

Our proposed framework consists of three main components:

A. Feature Extraction Module
We utilize a modified Vision Transformer
backbone pre-trained on LAION-5B for robust feature
extraction. The backbone is fine-tuned with a learning rate of 0.0005.

B. Classification Head
A 2-layer MLP with dropout (p=0.3) is attached
to the backbone output. We use Swish activations.

C. Training Objective
We optimize using AdamW with weight decay
0.0001. The loss function is cross-entropy combined with a
regularization term λ=0.001.

IV. EXPERIMENTS

Datasets: We evaluate on 3 public benchmarks.
Implementation: PyTorch 2.1, NVIDIA A100 GPU, batch size 32.

Results: Our method achieves 92.5% accuracy on the primary benchmark,
outperforming baselines by 4%. Inference time is 18ms per sample.

V. CONCLUSION

We presented a novel approach to transformer architecture for code generation demonstrating significant improvements over
state-of-the-art methods. Future work will explore multi-modal inputs.

REFERENCES
[1] A. Smith et al., "Deep Learning for NLP," IEEE CVPR, 2020.
[2] B. Lee et al., "Attention Mechanisms in GPT," NeurIPS, 2021.
[3] C. Wang et al., "Transfer Learning Survey," IEEE TPAMI, 2022.

FEDERATED LEARNING FOR PRIVACY-PRESERVING ML

Arjun Sharma¹, Sneha Iyer²
¹Department of Computer Science, IIT Delhi
²Department of Data Science, IIT Delhi

Abstract—
This paper presents a novel approach to federated learning for privacy-preserving ml using state-of-the-art machine learning
techniques. We propose a framework that addresses key challenges in Distributed AI by leveraging
privacy architectures. Experimental results on benchmark datasets demonstrate
that our method achieves superior performance compared to existing baselines, with an improvement
of 10% in accuracy and 29% reduction in computational overhead.
Our ablation study confirms the contribution of each component.

Keywords— privacy, gradient, federated, neural networks, distributed ai, benchmark evaluation

I. INTRODUCTION

The rapid advancement of artificial intelligence has enabled significant breakthroughs in Distributed AI.
Traditional approaches suffer from limitations including high computational cost, lack of scalability,
and poor generalization to unseen data. Recent works [1,2,3] have attempted to address these challenges
using deep learning, but remain constrained by data availability and model interpretability.

In this paper, we make the following contributions:
(1) We propose a novel privacy-based architecture optimized for Distributed AI
(2) We introduce a training strategy that reduces overfitting by 34%
(3) We conduct extensive experiments on 3 benchmark datasets
(4) We release our code and pre-trained models for reproducibility

II. RELATED WORK

Several approaches have been proposed for federated learning for privacy-preserving ml. Smith et al. [1] introduced a CNN-based
method achieving 79% accuracy on standard benchmarks. Lee et al. [2] proposed
an attention mechanism that improves feature extraction but requires 8x more
parameters. Wang et al. [3] applied transfer learning from ImageNet, achieving state-of-the-art
results on domain-specific tasks. Our work differs by addressing computational efficiency without
sacrificing performance.

III. METHODOLOGY

Our proposed framework consists of three main components:

A. Feature Extraction Module
We utilize a modified EfficientNet-B4
backbone pre-trained on ImageNet for robust feature
extraction. The backbone is fine-tuned with a learning rate of 0.0001.

B. Classification Head
A 4-layer MLP with dropout (p=0.3) is attached
to the backbone output. We use Swish activations.

C. Training Objective
We optimize using AdamW with weight decay
0.0001. The loss function is cross-entropy combined with a
regularization term λ=0.001.

IV. EXPERIMENTS

Datasets: We evaluate on 3 public benchmarks.
Implementation: PyTorch 2.1, NVIDIA A100 GPU, batch size 64.

Results: Our method achieves 94.8% accuracy on the primary benchmark,
outperforming baselines by 7%. Inference time is 28ms per sample.

V. CONCLUSION

We presented a novel approach to federated learning for privacy-preserving ml demonstrating significant improvements over
state-of-the-art methods. Future work will explore multi-modal inputs.

REFERENCES
[1] A. Smith et al., "Deep Learning for Distributed AI," IEEE CVPR, 2022.
[2] B. Lee et al., "Attention Mechanisms in privacy," NeurIPS, 2023.
[3] C. Wang et al., "Transfer Learning Survey," IEEE TPAMI, 2024.

SOFTWARE ENGINEERING — LECTURE NOTES
Unit 3: Design Patterns
Date: 12/1/2024

============================================================
CHAPTER OVERVIEW
============================================================

This unit covers fundamental concepts of MVC in the context of software engineering.
By the end of this unit, students should be able to:
• Explain the concept of MVC and its significance
• Differentiate between singleton and factory
• Apply theoretical concepts to solve problems
• Analyze real-world scenarios involving MVC

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TOPIC 1: INTRODUCTION TO MVC
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Definition:
A MVC is defined as an abstract data structure that maintains a hierarchical relationship.

Key Properties:
1. Efficiency — must satisfy all MVC requirements
2. Consistency — performance under increasing load
3. Durability — behavior in failure scenarios

Example:
Consider a system with 7 processes/nodes. The MVC operates
as follows:
Step 1: Initialize the MVC with default parameters
Step 2: Apply singleton to determine priority/order
Step 3: Execute the operation while maintaining factory invariants
Step 4: Verify correctness using post-conditions

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TOPIC 2: SINGLETON VS FACTORY
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Comparison Table:
┌─────────────────┬───────────────────────┬───────────────────────┐
│ Parameter        │ singleton             │ factory               │
├─────────────────┼───────────────────────┼───────────────────────┤
│ Complexity      │ O(n log n)            │ O(n²)                 │
│ Space           │ O(n)                  │ O(1)                  │
│ Use Case        │ Large datasets        │ Small datasets        │
│ Stability       │ Stable                │ Not stable            │
└─────────────────┴───────────────────────┴───────────────────────┘

Important Note: singleton is preferred when data is already partially sorted.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TOPIC 3: ALGORITHMS & COMPLEXITY ANALYSIS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Algorithm — Mvc:
```
function MVC(input):
    if input is empty:
        return null
    
    result = initialize()
    for each element in input:
        process(element)
        update(result)
    
    return result
```

Time Complexity: O(n)
Space Complexity: O(log n)
Best Case: O(1)
Worst Case: O(n)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PRACTICE QUESTIONS

OPERATING SYSTEMS — LECTURE NOTES
Unit 3: Process Management
Date: 24/1/2024

============================================================
CHAPTER OVERVIEW
============================================================

This unit covers fundamental concepts of process in the context of operating systems.
By the end of this unit, students should be able to:
• Explain the concept of process and its significance
• Differentiate between thread and scheduling
• Apply theoretical concepts to solve problems
• Analyze real-world scenarios involving process

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TOPIC 1: INTRODUCTION TO PROCESS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Definition:
A process is defined as an abstract data structure that maintains a hierarchical relationship.

Key Properties:
1. Correctness — must satisfy all process requirements
2. Consistency — performance under increasing load
3. Atomicity — behavior in failure scenarios

Example:
Consider a system with 4 processes/nodes. The process operates
as follows:
Step 1: Initialize the process with default parameters
Step 2: Apply thread to determine priority/order
Step 3: Execute the operation while maintaining scheduling invariants
Step 4: Verify correctness using post-conditions

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TOPIC 2: THREAD VS SCHEDULING
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Comparison Table:
┌─────────────────┬───────────────────────┬───────────────────────┐
│ Parameter        │ thread                │ scheduling            │
├─────────────────┼───────────────────────┼───────────────────────┤
│ Complexity      │ O(n log n)            │ O(n²)                 │
│ Space           │ O(n)                  │ O(1)                  │
│ Use Case        │ Large datasets        │ Small datasets        │
│ Stability       │ Stable                │ Not stable            │
└─────────────────┴───────────────────────┴───────────────────────┘

Important Note: thread is preferred when data is already partially sorted.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TOPIC 3: ALGORITHMS & COMPLEXITY ANALYSIS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Algorithm — Process:
```
function process(input):
    if input is empty:
        return null
    
    result = initialize()
    for each element in input:
        process(element)
        update(result)
    
    return result
```

Time Complexity: O(1)
Space Complexity: O(1)
Best Case: O(n)
Worst Case: O(n log n)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. Efficiency = (Useful Work Done) / (Total Resources Used)
2. Throughput = (Number of Processes Completed) / (Time Period)  
3. Waiting Time = Completion Time - Burst Time - Arrival Time
4. Turnaround Time = Completion Time - Arrival Time

text:
FEDERATED LEARNING FOR PRIVACY-PRESERVING ML

Amit Singh¹, Sneha Iyer²
¹Department of Computer Science, VIT Vellore
²Department of Data Science, IIIT Hyderabad

Abstract—
This paper presents a novel approach to federated learning for privacy-preserving ml using state-of-the-art machine learning
techniques. We propose a framework that addresses key challenges in Distributed AI by leveraging
privacy architectures. Experimental results on benchmark datasets demonstrate
that our method achieves superior performance compared to existing baselines, with an improvement
of 3% in accuracy and 37% reduction in computational overhead.
Our ablation study confirms the contribution of each component.

Keywords— privacy, gradient, federated, neural networks, distributed ai, benchmark evaluation

I. INTRODUCTION

The rapid advancement of artificial intelligence has enabled significant breakthroughs in Distributed AI.
Traditional approaches suffer from limitations including high computational cost, lack of scalability,
and poor generalization to unseen data. Recent works [1,2,3] have attempted to address these challenges
using deep learning, but remain constrained by data availability and model interpretability.

In this paper, we make the following contributions:
(1) We propose a novel privacy-based architecture optimized for Distributed AI
(2) We introduce a training strategy that reduces overfitting by 15%
(3) We conduct extensive experiments on 6 benchmark datasets
(4) We release our code and pre-trained models for reproducibility

II. RELATED WORK

Several approaches have been proposed for federated learning for privacy-preserving ml. Smith et al. [1] introduced a CNN-based
method achieving 77% accuracy on standard benchmarks. Lee et al. [2] proposed
an attention mechanism that improves feature extraction but requires 8x more
parameters. Wang et al. [3] applied transfer learning from ImageNet, achieving state-of-the-art
results on domain-specific tasks. Our work differs by addressing computational efficiency without
sacrificing performance.

III. METHODOLOGY

Our proposed framework consists of three main components:

A. Feature Extraction Module
We utilize a modified EfficientNet-B4
backbone pre-trained on LAION-5B for robust feature
extraction. The backbone is fine-tuned with a learning rate of 0.0001.

B. Classification Head
A 2-layer MLP with dropout (p=0.4) is attached
to the backbone output. We use Swish activations.

C. Training Objective
We optimize using SGD with Momentum with weight decay
5e-05. The loss function is cross-entropy combined with a
regularization term λ=0.1.

IV. EXPERIMENTS

Datasets: We evaluate on 4 public benchmarks.
Implementation: PyTorch 2.0, NVIDIA A100 GPU, batch size 32.

Results: Our method achieves 96.0% accuracy on the primary benchmark,
outperforming baselines by 8%. Inference time is 34ms per sample.

V. CONCLUSION

We presented a novel approach to federated learning for privacy-preserving ml demonstrating significant improvements over
state-of-the-art methods. Future work will explore real-time deployment.

REFERENCES
[1] A. Smith et al., "Deep Learning for Distributed AI," IEEE CVPR, 2022.
[2] B. Lee et al., "Attention Mechanisms in privacy," NeurIPS, 2023.
[3] C. Wang et al., "Transfer Learning Survey," IEEE TPAMI, 2024.
