MACHINE LEARNING — LECTURE NOTES
Unit 6: Neural Networks
Date: 25/1/2024

============================================================
CHAPTER OVERVIEW
============================================================

This unit covers fundamental concepts of perceptron in the context of machine learning.
By the end of this unit, students should be able to:
• Explain the concept of perceptron and its significance
• Differentiate between backpropagation and gradient descent
• Apply theoretical concepts to solve problems
• Analyze real-world scenarios involving perceptron

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TOPIC 1: INTRODUCTION TO PERCEPTRON
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Definition:
A perceptron is defined as an algorithm that systematically explores nodes in a graph structure.

Key Properties:
1. Efficiency — must satisfy all perceptron requirements
2. Scalability — performance under increasing load
3. Durability — behavior in failure scenarios

Example:
Consider a system with 5 processes/nodes. The perceptron operates
as follows:
Step 1: Initialize the perceptron with default parameters
Step 2: Apply backpropagation to determine priority/order
Step 3: Execute the operation while maintaining gradient descent invariants
Step 4: Verify correctness using post-conditions

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TOPIC 2: BACKPROPAGATION VS GRADIENT DESCENT
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Comparison Table:
┌─────────────────┬───────────────────────┬───────────────────────┐
│ Parameter        │ backpropagation       │ gradient descent      │
├─────────────────┼───────────────────────┼───────────────────────┤
│ Complexity      │ O(n log n)            │ O(n²)                 │
│ Space           │ O(n)                  │ O(1)                  │
│ Use Case        │ Large datasets        │ Small datasets        │
│ Stability       │ Stable                │ Not stable            │
└─────────────────┴───────────────────────┴───────────────────────┘

Important Note: backpropagation is preferred when memory is not a constraint and speed is critical.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TOPIC 3: ALGORITHMS & COMPLEXITY ANALYSIS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Algorithm — Perceptron:
```
function perceptron(input):
    if input is empty:
        return null
    
    result = initialize()
    for each element in input:
        process(element)
        update(result)
    
    return result
```

Time Complexity: O(n log n)
Space Complexity: O(n)
Best Case: O(log n)
Worst Case: O(n)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PRACTICE QUESTIONS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. [2 marks] Define perceptron and list its properties.

2. [4 marks] Compare and contrast backpropagation with gradient descent using suitable examples.

3. [6 marks] Given a scenario with 5 concurrent operations, trace the execution
   of perceptron step by step.

4. [8 marks] Design an efficient algorithm using perceptron for the following problem:
   [Problem description with constraints and expected output]

5. [Previous Year Question 2022] Explain the trade-offs between backpropagation and
   gradient descent. When would you choose one over the other? (8 marks)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
IMPORTANT FORMULAE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. Efficiency = (Useful Work Done) / (Total Resources Used)
2. Throughput = (Number of Processes Completed) / (Time Period)  
3. Waiting Time = Completion Time - Burst Time - Arrival Time
4. Turnaround Time = Completion Time - Arrival Time

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
REFERENCES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. Silberschatz, Galvin, Gagne — "Machine Learning", 10th Edition, Wiley
2. NPTEL Video Lectures — Machine Learning by Prof. Rohan Gupta, IIT Madras
3. GeeksForGeeks — perceptron tutorials

AIM / OBJECTIVE:
To verify and study stack implementation using arrays and to understand the relationship between
push and pop under controlled conditions.

Stack Implementation using Arrays is a fundamental concept in Data Structures. According to the theoretical framework,
push is directly proportional to pop
when overflow remains constant. This relationship can be expressed
mathematically and verified through controlled experimentation.

APPARATUS / MATERIALS REQUIRED:
1. Oscilloscope

3. Masses
4. Function generator

PROCEDURE:

2. Ensure all connections are secure and the circuit is properly grounded.
3. Gradually vary push from minimum to maximum range.
4. For each setting, record the corresponding pop.
5. Repeat each reading 2 times to minimize random errors.
6. Tabulate all observations carefully.
7. Plot the graph of push vs pop.

OBSERVATIONS:

    S.No.     push            pop            
    ────────────────────────────────────────
    1         8.55          1.87
    2         7.7          3.34
    3         4.24          6.02
    4         8.29          4.1
    5         4.71          4.16
    6         7.12          6.45
    7         6.19          6.85
    8         5.85          9.81

CALCULATIONS:
Mean value = 5.325
Standard Deviation = 2.316
Percentage Error = 2.02%

A graph of push vs pop was plotted.
The graph shows a sinusoidal relationship,
confirming the theoretical prediction.

The experiment successfully verified Stack Implementation using Arrays. The experimentally obtained value of
overflow = 46.911 mol/L
The theoretical value = 86.958
Percentage error = 3.24%

SOURCES OF ERROR:
1. Instrumental errors due to limited precision of measuring instruments
2. Random errors due to fluctuations in push
3. Human parallax error during reading

The experiment was performed successfully. The results confirm the theoretical relationship
between push and pop. The percentage
error of 4.65% is within acceptable limits, indicating accurate
experimental technique.

PRECAUTIONS:

text:
GRAPH NEURAL NETWORKS FOR FRAUD DETECTION

Neha Agarwal¹, Amit Singh²
¹Department of Computer Science, SRM University
²Department of Data Science, BITS Pilani

Abstract—
This paper presents a novel approach to graph neural networks for fraud detection using state-of-the-art machine learning
techniques. We propose a framework that addresses key challenges in FinTech by leveraging
GNN architectures. Experimental results on benchmark datasets demonstrate
that our method achieves superior performance compared to existing baselines, with an improvement
of 4% in accuracy and 10% reduction in computational overhead.
Our ablation study confirms the contribution of each component.

Keywords— GNN, node classification, transactions, neural networks, fintech, benchmark evaluation

I. INTRODUCTION

The rapid advancement of artificial intelligence has enabled significant breakthroughs in FinTech.
Traditional approaches suffer from limitations including high computational cost, lack of scalability,
and poor generalization to unseen data. Recent works [1,2,3] have attempted to address these challenges
using deep learning, but remain constrained by data availability and model interpretability.

In this paper, we make the following contributions:
(1) We propose a novel GNN-based architecture optimized for FinTech
(2) We introduce a training strategy that reduces overfitting by 21%
(3) We conduct extensive experiments on 4 benchmark datasets
(4) We release our code and pre-trained models for reproducibility

II. RELATED WORK

Several approaches have been proposed for graph neural networks for fraud detection. Smith et al. [1] introduced a CNN-based
method achieving 85% accuracy on standard benchmarks. Lee et al. [2] proposed
an attention mechanism that improves feature extraction but requires 5x more
parameters. Wang et al. [3] applied transfer learning from ImageNet, achieving state-of-the-art
results on domain-specific tasks. Our work differs by addressing computational efficiency without
sacrificing performance.

III. METHODOLOGY

Our proposed framework consists of three main components:

A. Feature Extraction Module
We utilize a modified ResNet-50
backbone pre-trained on JFT-300M for robust feature
extraction. The backbone is fine-tuned with a learning rate of 0.0001.

B. Classification Head
A 3-layer MLP with dropout (p=0.4) is attached
to the backbone output. We use GELU activations.

C. Training Objective
We optimize using SGD with Momentum with weight decay
5e-05. The loss function is cross-entropy combined with a
regularization term λ=0.001.

IV. EXPERIMENTS

Datasets: We evaluate on 2 public benchmarks.
Implementation: PyTorch 2.1, NVIDIA A100 GPU, batch size 64.

Results: Our method achieves 90.9% accuracy on the primary benchmark,
outperforming baselines by 4%. Inference time is 36ms per sample.

V. CONCLUSION

We presented a novel approach to graph neural networks for fraud detection demonstrating significant improvements over
state-of-the-art methods. Future work will explore real-time deployment.

REFERENCES
[1] A. Smith et al., "Deep Learning for FinTech," IEEE CVPR, 2022.
[2] B. Lee et al., "Attention Mechanisms in GNN," NeurIPS, 2023.
[3] C. Wang et al., "Transfer Learning Survey," IEEE TPAMI, 2024.

MACHINE LEARNING — LECTURE NOTES
Unit 6: Neural Networks
Date: 22/6/2024

============================================================
CHAPTER OVERVIEW
============================================================

This unit covers fundamental concepts of perceptron in the context of machine learning.
By the end of this unit, students should be able to:
• Explain the concept of perceptron and its significance
• Differentiate between backpropagation and gradient descent
• Apply theoretical concepts to solve problems
• Analyze real-world scenarios involving perceptron

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TOPIC 1: INTRODUCTION TO PERCEPTRON
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Definition:
A perceptron is defined as an abstract data structure that maintains a hierarchical relationship.

Key Properties:
1. Efficiency — must satisfy all perceptron requirements
2. Reliability — performance under increasing load
3. Durability — behavior in failure scenarios

Example:
Consider a system with 6 processes/nodes. The perceptron operates
as follows:
Step 1: Initialize the perceptron with default parameters
Step 2: Apply backpropagation to determine priority/order
Step 3: Execute the operation while maintaining gradient descent invariants
Step 4: Verify correctness using post-conditions

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TOPIC 2: BACKPROPAGATION VS GRADIENT DESCENT
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Comparison Table:
┌─────────────────┬───────────────────────┬───────────────────────┐
│ Parameter        │ backpropagation       │ gradient descent      │
├─────────────────┼───────────────────────┼───────────────────────┤
│ Complexity      │ O(n log n)            │ O(n²)                 │
│ Space           │ O(n)                  │ O(1)                  │
│ Use Case        │ Large datasets        │ Small datasets        │
│ Stability       │ Stable                │ Not stable            │
└─────────────────┴───────────────────────┴───────────────────────┘

Important Note: backpropagation is preferred when stability of equal elements must be preserved.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TOPIC 3: ALGORITHMS & COMPLEXITY ANALYSIS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Algorithm — Perceptron:
```
function perceptron(input):
    if input is empty:
        return null
    
    result = initialize()
    for each element in input:
        process(element)
        update(result)
    
    return result
```

Time Complexity: O(n log n)
Space Complexity: O(1)
Best Case: O(log n)
Worst Case: O(n²)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

TRANSFORMER ARCHITECTURE FOR CODE GENERATION

Ananya Das¹, Suresh Pillai²
¹Department of Computer Science, NIT Trichy
²Department of Data Science, VIT Vellore

Abstract—
This paper presents a novel approach to transformer architecture for code generation using state-of-the-art machine learning
techniques. We propose a framework that addresses key challenges in NLP by leveraging
GPT architectures. Experimental results on benchmark datasets demonstrate
that our method achieves superior performance compared to existing baselines, with an improvement
of 13% in accuracy and 12% reduction in computational overhead.
Our ablation study confirms the contribution of each component.

Keywords— GPT, code synthesis, program generation, neural networks, nlp, benchmark evaluation

I. INTRODUCTION

The rapid advancement of artificial intelligence has enabled significant breakthroughs in NLP.
Traditional approaches suffer from limitations including high computational cost, lack of scalability,
and poor generalization to unseen data. Recent works [1,2,3] have attempted to address these challenges
using deep learning, but remain constrained by data availability and model interpretability.

In this paper, we make the following contributions:
(1) We propose a novel GPT-based architecture optimized for NLP
(2) We introduce a training strategy that reduces overfitting by 22%
(3) We conduct extensive experiments on 5 benchmark datasets
(4) We release our code and pre-trained models for reproducibility

II. RELATED WORK

Several approaches have been proposed for transformer architecture for code generation. Smith et al. [1] introduced a CNN-based
method achieving 87% accuracy on standard benchmarks. Lee et al. [2] proposed
an attention mechanism that improves feature extraction but requires 8x more
parameters. Wang et al. [3] applied transfer learning from ImageNet, achieving state-of-the-art
results on domain-specific tasks. Our work differs by addressing computational efficiency without
sacrificing performance.

III. METHODOLOGY

Our proposed framework consists of three main components:

A. Feature Extraction Module
We utilize a modified ResNet-50
backbone pre-trained on LAION-5B for robust feature
extraction. The backbone is fine-tuned with a learning rate of 0.0005.

B. Classification Head
A 2-layer MLP with dropout (p=0.5) is attached
to the backbone output. We use Swish activations.

C. Training Objective
We optimize using AdaFactor with weight decay
0.0001. The loss function is cross-entropy combined with a
regularization term λ=0.1.

IV. EXPERIMENTS

Datasets: We evaluate on 3 public benchmarks.
Implementation: PyTorch 2.0, NVIDIA A100 GPU, batch size 64.

Results: Our method achieves 87.5% accuracy on the primary benchmark,
outperforming baselines by 8%. Inference time is 27ms per sample.

V. CONCLUSION

We presented a novel approach to transformer architecture for code generation demonstrating significant improvements over
state-of-the-art methods. Future work will explore real-time deployment.

REFERENCES
[1] A. Smith et al., "Deep Learning for NLP," IEEE CVPR, 2020.
[2] B. Lee et al., "Attention Mechanisms in GPT," NeurIPS, 2021.
[3] C. Wang et al., "Transfer Learning Survey," IEEE TPAMI, 2022.

COMPUTER NETWORKS — LECTURE NOTES
Unit 5: Transport Layer
Date: 4/9/2024

============================================================
CHAPTER OVERVIEW
============================================================

This unit covers fundamental concepts of TCP in the context of computer networks.
By the end of this unit, students should be able to:
• Explain the concept of TCP and its significance
• Differentiate between UDP and socket
• Apply theoretical concepts to solve problems
• Analyze real-world scenarios involving TCP

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TOPIC 1: INTRODUCTION TO TCP
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Definition:
A TCP is defined as a mechanism for managing shared resources in a concurrent environment.

Key Properties:
1. Correctness — must satisfy all TCP requirements
2. Reliability — performance under increasing load
3. Isolation — behavior in failure scenarios

Example:
Consider a system with 5 processes/nodes. The TCP operates
as follows:
Step 1: Initialize the TCP with default parameters
Step 2: Apply UDP to determine priority/order
Step 3: Execute the operation while maintaining socket invariants
Step 4: Verify correctness using post-conditions

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TOPIC 2: UDP VS SOCKET
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Comparison Table:
┌─────────────────┬───────────────────────┬───────────────────────┐
│ Parameter        │ UDP                   │ socket                │
├─────────────────┼───────────────────────┼───────────────────────┤
│ Complexity      │ O(n log n)            │ O(n²)                 │
│ Space           │ O(n)                  │ O(1)                  │
│ Use Case        │ Large datasets        │ Small datasets        │
│ Stability       │ Stable                │ Not stable            │
└─────────────────┴───────────────────────┴───────────────────────┘

Important Note: UDP is preferred when stability of equal elements must be preserved.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TOPIC 3: ALGORITHMS & COMPLEXITY ANALYSIS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Algorithm — Tcp:
```
function TCP(input):
    if input is empty:
        return null
    
    result = initialize()
    for each element in input:
        process(element)
        update(result)
    
    return result
```

Time Complexity: O(n log n)
Space Complexity: O(n)
Best Case: O(log n)

DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS — LECTURE NOTES
Unit 4: SQL and Transactions
Date: 9/2/2024

============================================================
CHAPTER OVERVIEW
============================================================

This unit covers fundamental concepts of SQL in the context of database management systems.
By the end of this unit, students should be able to:
• Explain the concept of SQL and its significance
• Differentiate between ACID and transaction
• Apply theoretical concepts to solve problems
• Analyze real-world scenarios involving SQL

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TOPIC 1: INTRODUCTION TO SQL
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Definition:
A SQL is defined as an algorithm that systematically explores nodes in a graph structure.

Key Properties:
1. Correctness — must satisfy all SQL requirements
2. Scalability — performance under increasing load
3. Durability — behavior in failure scenarios

Example:
Consider a system with 3 processes/nodes. The SQL operates
as follows:
Step 1: Initialize the SQL with default parameters
Step 2: Apply ACID to determine priority/order
Step 3: Execute the operation while maintaining transaction invariants
Step 4: Verify correctness using post-conditions

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TOPIC 2: ACID VS TRANSACTION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Comparison Table:
┌─────────────────┬───────────────────────┬───────────────────────┐
│ Parameter        │ ACID                  │ transaction           │
├─────────────────┼───────────────────────┼───────────────────────┤
│ Complexity      │ O(n log n)            │ O(n²)                 │
│ Space           │ O(n)                  │ O(1)                  │
│ Use Case        │ Large datasets        │ Small datasets        │
│ Stability       │ Stable                │ Not stable            │
└─────────────────┴───────────────────────┴───────────────────────┘

Important Note: ACID is preferred when stability of equal elements must be preserved.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TOPIC 3: ALGORITHMS & COMPLEXITY ANALYSIS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Algorithm — Sql:
```
function SQL(input):
    if input is empty:
        return null
    
    result = initialize()
    for each element in input:
        process(element)
        update(result)
    
    return result
```

Time Complexity: O(n)
Space Complexity: O(1)
Best Case: O(log n)
Worst Case: O(n)

REINFORCEMENT LEARNING FOR AUTONOMOUS VEHICLES

Ravi Bhat¹, Divya Kumar²
¹Department of Computer Science, IIT Bombay
²Department of Data Science, SRM University

Abstract—
This paper presents a novel approach to reinforcement learning for autonomous vehicles using state-of-the-art machine learning
techniques. We propose a framework that addresses key challenges in Robotics by leveraging
reward architectures. Experimental results on benchmark datasets demonstrate
that our method achieves superior performance compared to existing baselines, with an improvement
of 5% in accuracy and 26% reduction in computational overhead.
Our ablation study confirms the contribution of each component.

Keywords— reward, policy, self-driving, neural networks, robotics, benchmark evaluation

I. INTRODUCTION

The rapid advancement of artificial intelligence has enabled significant breakthroughs in Robotics.
Traditional approaches suffer from limitations including high computational cost, lack of scalability,
and poor generalization to unseen data. Recent works [1,2,3] have attempted to address these challenges
using deep learning, but remain constrained by data availability and model interpretability.

In this paper, we make the following contributions:
(1) We propose a novel reward-based architecture optimized for Robotics
(2) We introduce a training strategy that reduces overfitting by 30%
(3) We conduct extensive experiments on 6 benchmark datasets
(4) We release our code and pre-trained models for reproducibility

II. RELATED WORK

Several approaches have been proposed for reinforcement learning for autonomous vehicles. Smith et al. [1] introduced a CNN-based
method achieving 88% accuracy on standard benchmarks. Lee et al. [2] proposed
an attention mechanism that improves feature extraction but requires 4x more
parameters. Wang et al. [3] applied transfer learning from ImageNet, achieving state-of-the-art
results on domain-specific tasks. Our work differs by addressing computational efficiency without
sacrificing performance.

III. METHODOLOGY

Our proposed framework consists of three main components:

A. Feature Extraction Module
We utilize a modified ResNet-50
backbone pre-trained on JFT-300M for robust feature
extraction. The backbone is fine-tuned with a learning rate of 0.0005.

B. Classification Head

to the backbone output. We use GELU activations.

C. Training Objective
We optimize using AdamW with weight decay
5e-05. The loss function is cross-entropy combined with a
regularization term λ=0.01.

IV. EXPERIMENTS

Datasets: We evaluate on 4 public benchmarks.
Implementation: PyTorch 2.1, NVIDIA A100 GPU, batch size 32.

Results: Our method achieves 91.0% accuracy on the primary benchmark,
outperforming baselines by 3%. Inference time is 17ms per sample.

SOFTWARE ENGINEERING — LECTURE NOTES
Unit 3: Design Patterns
Date: 4/7/2024

============================================================
CHAPTER OVERVIEW
============================================================

This unit covers fundamental concepts of MVC in the context of software engineering.
By the end of this unit, students should be able to:
• Explain the concept of MVC and its significance
• Differentiate between singleton and factory
• Apply theoretical concepts to solve problems
• Analyze real-world scenarios involving MVC

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TOPIC 1: INTRODUCTION TO MVC
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Definition:
A MVC is defined as an algorithm that systematically explores nodes in a graph structure.

Key Properties:
1. Completeness — must satisfy all MVC requirements
2. Consistency — performance under increasing load
3. Isolation — behavior in failure scenarios

Example:
Consider a system with 3 processes/nodes. The MVC operates
as follows:
Step 1: Initialize the MVC with default parameters
Step 2: Apply singleton to determine priority/order
Step 3: Execute the operation while maintaining factory invariants
Step 4: Verify correctness using post-conditions

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TOPIC 2: SINGLETON VS FACTORY
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Comparison Table:
┌─────────────────┬───────────────────────┬───────────────────────┐
│ Parameter        │ singleton             │ factory               │
├─────────────────┼───────────────────────┼───────────────────────┤
│ Complexity      │ O(n log n)            │ O(n²)                 │
│ Space           │ O(n)                  │ O(1)                  │
│ Use Case        │ Large datasets        │ Small datasets        │
│ Stability       │ Stable                │ Not stable            │
└─────────────────┴───────────────────────┴───────────────────────┘

Important Note: singleton is preferred when memory is not a constraint and speed is critical.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TOPIC 3: ALGORITHMS & COMPLEXITY ANALYSIS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Algorithm — Mvc:
```
function MVC(input):
    if input is empty:
        return null
    
    result = initialize()
    for each element in input:
        process(element)
        update(result)
    
    return result
```

Time Complexity: O(n²)
Space Complexity: O(n²)
Best Case: O(log n)
Worst Case: O(n)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PRACTICE QUESTIONS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. [2 marks] Define MVC and list its properties.

2. [4 marks] Compare and contrast singleton with factory using suitable examples.

3. [6 marks] Given a scenario with 7 concurrent operations, trace the execution
   of MVC step by step.

4. [8 marks] Design an efficient algorithm using MVC for the following problem:
   [Problem description with constraints and expected output]

5. [Previous Year Question 2022] Explain the trade-offs between singleton and
   factory. When would you choose one over the other? (8 marks)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
IMPORTANT FORMULAE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. Efficiency = (Useful Work Done) / (Total Resources Used)
2. Throughput = (Number of Processes Completed) / (Time Period)  
3. Waiting Time = Completion Time - Burst Time - Arrival Time
4. Turnaround Time = Completion Time - Arrival Time

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
REFERENCES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. Silberschatz, Galvin, Gagne — "Software Engineering", 10th Edition, Wiley
2. NPTEL Video Lectures — Software Engineering by Prof. Priya Patel, IIT Madras
3. GeeksForGeeks — MVC tutorials
4. Previous year question papers — NIT Trichy 2018

GRAPH NEURAL NETWORKS FOR FRAUD DETECTION

Neha Agarwal¹, Suresh Pillai²
¹Department of Computer Science, VIT Vellore
²Department of Data Science, VIT Vellore

Abstract—
This paper presents a novel approach to graph neural networks for fraud detection using state-of-the-art machine learning
techniques. We propose a framework that addresses key challenges in FinTech by leveraging
GNN architectures. Experimental results on benchmark datasets demonstrate
that our method achieves superior performance compared to existing baselines, with an improvement
of 10% in accuracy and 37% reduction in computational overhead.
Our ablation study confirms the contribution of each component.

Keywords— GNN, node classification, transactions, neural networks, fintech, benchmark evaluation

I. INTRODUCTION

The rapid advancement of artificial intelligence has enabled significant breakthroughs in FinTech.
Traditional approaches suffer from limitations including high computational cost, lack of scalability,
and poor generalization to unseen data. Recent works [1,2,3] have attempted to address these challenges
using deep learning, but remain constrained by data availability and model interpretability.

In this paper, we make the following contributions:
(1) We propose a novel GNN-based architecture optimized for FinTech
(2) We introduce a training strategy that reduces overfitting by 18%
(3) We conduct extensive experiments on 4 benchmark datasets
(4) We release our code and pre-trained models for reproducibility

II. RELATED WORK

Several approaches have been proposed for graph neural networks for fraud detection. Smith et al. [1] introduced a CNN-based
method achieving 77% accuracy on standard benchmarks. Lee et al. [2] proposed
an attention mechanism that improves feature extraction but requires 5x more
parameters. Wang et al. [3] applied transfer learning from ImageNet, achieving state-of-the-art
results on domain-specific tasks. Our work differs by addressing computational efficiency without
sacrificing performance.

III. METHODOLOGY

Our proposed framework consists of three main components:

A. Feature Extraction Module
We utilize a modified MobileNetV3
backbone pre-trained on JFT-300M for robust feature
extraction. The backbone is fine-tuned with a learning rate of 0.0005.

B. Classification Head
A 4-layer MLP with dropout (p=0.4) is attached
to the backbone output. We use Swish activations.

C. Training Objective
We optimize using AdaFactor with weight decay
0.0001. The loss function is cross-entropy combined with a
regularization term λ=0.01.

IV. EXPERIMENTS

Datasets: We evaluate on 3 public benchmarks.
Implementation: PyTorch 1.12, NVIDIA A100 GPU, batch size 32.

Results: Our method achieves 94.4% accuracy on the primary benchmark,
outperforming baselines by 8%. Inference time is 45ms per sample.

V. CONCLUSION

We presented a novel approach to graph neural networks for fraud detection demonstrating significant improvements over
state-of-the-art methods. Future work will explore real-time deployment.

REFERENCES
[1] A. Smith et al., "Deep Learning for FinTech," IEEE CVPR, 2021.
[2] B. Lee et al., "Attention Mechanisms in GNN," NeurIPS, 2022.
[3] C. Wang et al., "Transfer Learning Survey," IEEE TPAMI, 2023.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TOPIC 1: INTRODUCTION TO PERCEPTRON
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Definition:
A perceptron is defined as an abstract data structure that maintains a hierarchical relationship.

Key Properties:
1. Efficiency — must satisfy all perceptron requirements
2. Reliability — performance under increasing load
3. Atomicity — behavior in failure scenarios

Example:
Consider a system with 3 processes/nodes. The perceptron operates
as follows:
Step 1: Initialize the perceptron with default parameters
Step 2: Apply backpropagation to determine priority/order
Step 3: Execute the operation while maintaining gradient descent invariants
Step 4: Verify correctness using post-conditions

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TOPIC 2: BACKPROPAGATION VS GRADIENT DESCENT
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Comparison Table:
┌─────────────────┬───────────────────────┬───────────────────────┐
│ Parameter        │ backpropagation       │ gradient descent      │
├─────────────────┼───────────────────────┼───────────────────────┤
│ Complexity      │ O(n log n)            │ O(n²)                 │
│ Space           │ O(n)                  │ O(1)                  │
│ Use Case        │ Large datasets        │ Small datasets        │
│ Stability       │ Stable                │ Not stable            │
└─────────────────┴───────────────────────┴───────────────────────┘

Important Note: backpropagation is preferred when data is already partially sorted.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TOPIC 3: ALGORITHMS & COMPLEXITY ANALYSIS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Algorithm — Perceptron:
```
function perceptron(input):
    if input is empty:
        return null
    
    result = initialize()
    for each element in input:
        process(element)
        update(result)
    
    return result
```

Time Complexity: O(n²)
Space Complexity: O(1)
Best Case: O(n)
Worst Case: O(n²)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PRACTICE QUESTIONS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. [2 marks] Define perceptron and list its properties.

2. [4 marks] Compare and contrast backpropagation with gradient descent using suitable examples.

3. [6 marks] Given a scenario with 4 concurrent operations, trace the execution
   of perceptron step by step.

4. [8 marks] Design an efficient algorithm using perceptron for the following problem:
   [Problem description with constraints and expected output]

5. [Previous Year Question 2022] Explain the trade-offs between backpropagation and
   gradient descent. When would you choose one over the other? (8 marks)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
IMPORTANT FORMULAE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. Efficiency = (Useful Work Done) / (Total Resources Used)
2. Throughput = (Number of Processes Completed) / (Time Period)  
3. Waiting Time = Completion Time - Burst Time - Arrival Time
4. Turnaround Time = Completion Time - Arrival Time

Stack Implementation using Arrays is a fundamental concept in Data Structures. According to the theoretical framework,
push is directly proportional to pop
when overflow remains constant. This relationship can be expressed
mathematically and verified through controlled experimentation.

2. Spring balance

PROCEDURE:

2. Ensure all connections are secure and the circuit is properly grounded.
3. Gradually vary push from minimum to maximum range.
4. For each setting, record the corresponding pop.
5. Repeat each reading 3 times to minimize random errors.
6. Tabulate all observations carefully.
7. Plot the graph of push vs pop.

OBSERVATIONS:

    S.No.     push            pop            
    ────────────────────────────────────────
    1         3.05          3.85
    2         3.62          3.41
    3         6.8          3.44
    4         5.0          8.77
    5         4.27          6.28
    6         9.69          4.73
    7         2.65          1.21

CALCULATIONS:
Mean value = 4.527
Standard Deviation = 2.236
Percentage Error = 0.91%

GRAPH:
A graph of push vs pop was plotted.
The graph shows a linear relationship,
confirming the theoretical prediction.

RESULT:
The experiment successfully verified Stack Implementation using Arrays. The experimentally obtained value of
overflow = 93.743 Hz
The theoretical value = 55.295
Percentage error = 0.23%

SOURCES OF ERROR:

2. Random errors due to fluctuations in push

between push and pop. The percentage
error of 2.05% is within acceptable limits, indicating accurate

DEEP LEARNING FOR MEDICAL IMAGE SEGMENTATION

Karthik Reddy¹, Sneha Iyer²
¹Department of Computer Science, Amity University
²Department of Data Science, SRM University

Abstract—
This paper presents a novel approach to deep learning for medical image segmentation using state-of-the-art machine learning
techniques. We propose a framework that addresses key challenges in Healthcare AI by leveraging
CNN architectures. Experimental results on benchmark datasets demonstrate
that our method achieves superior performance compared to existing baselines, with an improvement
of 7% in accuracy and 15% reduction in computational overhead.
Our ablation study confirms the contribution of each component.

Keywords— CNN, U-Net, MRI, segmentation, neural networks, healthcare ai, benchmark evaluation

I. INTRODUCTION

The rapid advancement of artificial intelligence has enabled significant breakthroughs in Healthcare AI.
Traditional approaches suffer from limitations including high computational cost, lack of scalability,
and poor generalization to unseen data. Recent works [1,2,3] have attempted to address these challenges
using deep learning, but remain constrained by data availability and model interpretability.

In this paper, we make the following contributions:
(1) We propose a novel CNN-based architecture optimized for Healthcare AI
(2) We introduce a training strategy that reduces overfitting by 23%
(3) We conduct extensive experiments on 3 benchmark datasets
(4) We release our code and pre-trained models for reproducibility

II. RELATED WORK

Several approaches have been proposed for deep learning for medical image segmentation. Smith et al. [1] introduced a CNN-based
method achieving 85% accuracy on standard benchmarks. Lee et al. [2] proposed
an attention mechanism that improves feature extraction but requires 5x more
parameters. Wang et al. [3] applied transfer learning from ImageNet, achieving state-of-the-art
results on domain-specific tasks. Our work differs by addressing computational efficiency without
sacrificing performance.

III. METHODOLOGY

Our proposed framework consists of three main components:

A. Feature Extraction Module
We utilize a modified Vision Transformer
backbone pre-trained on ImageNet for robust feature
extraction. The backbone is fine-tuned with a learning rate of 0.0005.

B. Classification Head
A 4-layer MLP with dropout (p=0.5) is attached
to the backbone output. We use ReLU activations.

C. Training Objective
We optimize using AdamW with weight decay
1e-05. The loss function is cross-entropy combined with a
regularization term λ=0.1.

IV. EXPERIMENTS

Datasets: We evaluate on 3 public benchmarks.
Implementation: PyTorch 2.1, NVIDIA A100 GPU, batch size 64.

Results: Our method achieves 94.6% accuracy on the primary benchmark,
outperforming baselines by 5%. Inference time is 17ms per sample.

V. CONCLUSION

We presented a novel approach to deep learning for medical image segmentation demonstrating significant improvements over
state-of-the-art methods. Future work will explore zero-shot generalization.

REFERENCES
[1] A. Smith et al., "Deep Learning for Healthcare AI," IEEE CVPR, 2020.
[2] B. Lee et al., "Attention Mechanisms in CNN," NeurIPS, 2021.
[3] C. Wang et al., "Transfer Learning Survey," IEEE TPAMI, 2022.

GRAPH NEURAL NETWORKS FOR FRAUD DETECTION

Vikram Nair¹, Meera Joshi²
¹Department of Computer Science, Amity University
²Department of Data Science, Anna University

Abstract—
This paper presents a novel approach to graph neural networks for fraud detection using state-of-the-art machine learning
techniques. We propose a framework that addresses key challenges in FinTech by leveraging
GNN architectures. Experimental results on benchmark datasets demonstrate
that our method achieves superior performance compared to existing baselines, with an improvement
of 13% in accuracy and 14% reduction in computational overhead.
Our ablation study confirms the contribution of each component.

Keywords— GNN, node classification, transactions, neural networks, fintech, benchmark evaluation

I. INTRODUCTION

The rapid advancement of artificial intelligence has enabled significant breakthroughs in FinTech.
Traditional approaches suffer from limitations including high computational cost, lack of scalability,
and poor generalization to unseen data. Recent works [1,2,3] have attempted to address these challenges
using deep learning, but remain constrained by data availability and model interpretability.

In this paper, we make the following contributions:
(1) We propose a novel GNN-based architecture optimized for FinTech
(2) We introduce a training strategy that reduces overfitting by 25%
(3) We conduct extensive experiments on 6 benchmark datasets
(4) We release our code and pre-trained models for reproducibility

II. RELATED WORK

Several approaches have been proposed for graph neural networks for fraud detection. Smith et al. [1] introduced a CNN-based
method achieving 80% accuracy on standard benchmarks. Lee et al. [2] proposed
an attention mechanism that improves feature extraction but requires 4x more
parameters. Wang et al. [3] applied transfer learning from ImageNet, achieving state-of-the-art
results on domain-specific tasks. Our work differs by addressing computational efficiency without
sacrificing performance.

III. METHODOLOGY

Our proposed framework consists of three main components:

A. Feature Extraction Module
We utilize a modified EfficientNet-B4
backbone pre-trained on JFT-300M for robust feature
extraction. The backbone is fine-tuned with a learning rate of 0.0001.

B. Classification Head
A 2-layer MLP with dropout (p=0.3) is attached
to the backbone output. We use Swish activations.

C. Training Objective
We optimize using AdamW with weight decay
0.0001. The loss function is cross-entropy combined with a
regularization term λ=0.1.

IV. EXPERIMENTS

Datasets: We evaluate on 2 public benchmarks.
Implementation: PyTorch 2.1, NVIDIA A100 GPU, batch size 64.

Results: Our method achieves 88.7% accuracy on the primary benchmark,
outperforming baselines by 4%. Inference time is 14ms per sample.

V. CONCLUSION

We presented a novel approach to graph neural networks for fraud detection demonstrating significant improvements over
state-of-the-art methods. Future work will explore real-time deployment.

REFERENCES
[1] A. Smith et al., "Deep Learning for FinTech," IEEE CVPR, 2021.
[2] B. Lee et al., "Attention Mechanisms in GNN," NeurIPS, 2022.
[3] C. Wang et al., "Transfer Learning Survey," IEEE TPAMI, 2023.

text:
MACHINE LEARNING — LECTURE NOTES
Unit 6: Neural Networks
Date: 13/10/2024

============================================================
CHAPTER OVERVIEW
============================================================

This unit covers fundamental concepts of perceptron in the context of machine learning.
By the end of this unit, students should be able to:
• Explain the concept of perceptron and its significance
• Differentiate between backpropagation and gradient descent
• Apply theoretical concepts to solve problems
• Analyze real-world scenarios involving perceptron

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TOPIC 1: INTRODUCTION TO PERCEPTRON
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Definition:
A perceptron is defined as a protocol that ensures reliable data transmission over unreliable channels.

Key Properties:
1. Correctness — must satisfy all perceptron requirements
2. Reliability — performance under increasing load
3. Atomicity — behavior in failure scenarios

Example:
Consider a system with 3 processes/nodes. The perceptron operates
as follows:
Step 1: Initialize the perceptron with default parameters
Step 2: Apply backpropagation to determine priority/order
Step 3: Execute the operation while maintaining gradient descent invariants
Step 4: Verify correctness using post-conditions

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TOPIC 2: BACKPROPAGATION VS GRADIENT DESCENT
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Comparison Table:
┌─────────────────┬───────────────────────┬───────────────────────┐
│ Parameter        │ backpropagation       │ gradient descent      │
├─────────────────┼───────────────────────┼───────────────────────┤
│ Complexity      │ O(n log n)            │ O(n²)                 │
│ Space           │ O(n)                  │ O(1)                  │
│ Use Case        │ Large datasets        │ Small datasets        │
│ Stability       │ Stable                │ Not stable            │
└─────────────────┴───────────────────────┴───────────────────────┘

Important Note: backpropagation is preferred when stability of equal elements must be preserved.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TOPIC 3: ALGORITHMS & COMPLEXITY ANALYSIS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Algorithm — Perceptron:
```
function perceptron(input):
    if input is empty:
        return null
    
    result = initialize()
    for each element in input:
        process(element)
        update(result)
    
    return result
```

Time Complexity: O(n log n)
Space Complexity: O(log n)
Best Case: O(log n)
Worst Case: O(n log n)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PRACTICE QUESTIONS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. [2 marks] Define perceptron and list its properties.

2. [4 marks] Compare and contrast backpropagation with gradient descent using suitable examples.

3. [6 marks] Given a scenario with 7 concurrent operations, trace the execution
   of perceptron step by step.

4. [8 marks] Design an efficient algorithm using perceptron for the following problem:
   [Problem description with constraints and expected output]

5. [Previous Year Question 2022] Explain the trade-offs between backpropagation and
   gradient descent. When would you choose one over the other? (8 marks)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
IMPORTANT FORMULAE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. Efficiency = (Useful Work Done) / (Total Resources Used)
2. Throughput = (Number of Processes Completed) / (Time Period)  
3. Waiting Time = Completion Time - Burst Time - Arrival Time
4. Turnaround Time = Completion Time - Arrival Time

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
REFERENCES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. Silberschatz, Galvin, Gagne — "Machine Learning", 10th Edition, Wiley
2. NPTEL Video Lectures — Machine Learning by Prof. Arjun Sharma, IIT Madras
3. GeeksForGeeks — perceptron tutorials
4. Previous year question papers — Pune University 2021

text:
GRAPH NEURAL NETWORKS FOR FRAUD DETECTION

Suresh Pillai¹, Rahul Verma²
¹Department of Computer Science, Manipal Institute of Technology
²Department of Data Science, Manipal Institute of Technology

Abstract—
This paper presents a novel approach to graph neural networks for fraud detection using state-of-the-art machine learning
techniques. We propose a framework that addresses key challenges in FinTech by leveraging
GNN architectures. Experimental results on benchmark datasets demonstrate
that our method achieves superior performance compared to existing baselines, with an improvement
of 3% in accuracy and 31% reduction in computational overhead.
Our ablation study confirms the contribution of each component.

Keywords— GNN, node classification, transactions, neural networks, fintech, benchmark evaluation

I. INTRODUCTION

The rapid advancement of artificial intelligence has enabled significant breakthroughs in FinTech.
Traditional approaches suffer from limitations including high computational cost, lack of scalability,
and poor generalization to unseen data. Recent works [1,2,3] have attempted to address these challenges
using deep learning, but remain constrained by data availability and model interpretability.

In this paper, we make the following contributions:
(1) We propose a novel GNN-based architecture optimized for FinTech
(2) We introduce a training strategy that reduces overfitting by 18%
(3) We conduct extensive experiments on 3 benchmark datasets
(4) We release our code and pre-trained models for reproducibility

II. RELATED WORK

Several approaches have been proposed for graph neural networks for fraud detection. Smith et al. [1] introduced a CNN-based
method achieving 75% accuracy on standard benchmarks. Lee et al. [2] proposed
an attention mechanism that improves feature extraction but requires 8x more
parameters. Wang et al. [3] applied transfer learning from ImageNet, achieving state-of-the-art
results on domain-specific tasks. Our work differs by addressing computational efficiency without
sacrificing performance.

III. METHODOLOGY

Our proposed framework consists of three main components:

A. Feature Extraction Module
We utilize a modified ResNet-50
backbone pre-trained on JFT-300M for robust feature
extraction. The backbone is fine-tuned with a learning rate of 0.0005.

B. Classification Head
A 3-layer MLP with dropout (p=0.3) is attached
to the backbone output. We use Swish activations.

C. Training Objective
We optimize using SGD with Momentum with weight decay
1e-05. The loss function is cross-entropy combined with a
regularization term λ=0.1.

IV. EXPERIMENTS

Datasets: We evaluate on 2 public benchmarks.
Implementation: PyTorch 1.12, NVIDIA A100 GPU, batch size 64.

Results: Our method achieves 93.9% accuracy on the primary benchmark,
outperforming baselines by 2%. Inference time is 31ms per sample.

V. CONCLUSION

We presented a novel approach to graph neural networks for fraud detection demonstrating significant improvements over
state-of-the-art methods. Future work will explore real-time deployment.

REFERENCES
[1] A. Smith et al., "Deep Learning for FinTech," IEEE CVPR, 2019.
[2] B. Lee et al., "Attention Mechanisms in GNN," NeurIPS, 2020.
[3] C. Wang et al., "Transfer Learning Survey," IEEE TPAMI, 2021.

Step 1: Initialize the perceptron with default parameters
Step 2: Apply backpropagation to determine priority/order
Step 3: Execute the operation while maintaining gradient descent invariants
Step 4: Verify correctness using post-conditions

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TOPIC 2: BACKPROPAGATION VS GRADIENT DESCENT
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Comparison Table:
┌─────────────────┬───────────────────────┬───────────────────────┐
│ Parameter        │ backpropagation       │ gradient descent      │
├─────────────────┼───────────────────────┼───────────────────────┤
│ Complexity      │ O(n log n)            │ O(n²)                 │
│ Space           │ O(n)                  │ O(1)                  │
│ Use Case        │ Large datasets        │ Small datasets        │
│ Stability       │ Stable                │ Not stable            │
└─────────────────┴───────────────────────┴───────────────────────┘

Important Note: backpropagation is preferred when memory is not a constraint and speed is critical.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TOPIC 3: ALGORITHMS & COMPLEXITY ANALYSIS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Algorithm — Perceptron:
```
function perceptron(input):
    if input is empty:
        return null
    
    result = initialize()
    for each element in input:
        process(element)
        update(result)
    
    return result
```

Time Complexity: O(log n)
Space Complexity: O(1)
Best Case: O(log n)
Worst Case: O(n²)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PRACTICE QUESTIONS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. [2 marks] Define perceptron and list its properties.

2. [4 marks] Compare and contrast backpropagation with gradient descent using suitable examples.

3. [6 marks] Given a scenario with 5 concurrent operations, trace the execution
   of perceptron step by step.

4. [8 marks] Design an efficient algorithm using perceptron for the following problem:
   [Problem description with constraints and expected output]

5. [Previous Year Question 2022] Explain the trade-offs between backpropagation and
   gradient descent. When would you choose one over the other? (8 marks)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
IMPORTANT FORMULAE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. Efficiency = (Useful Work Done) / (Total Resources Used)
2. Throughput = (Number of Processes Completed) / (Time Period)  
3. Waiting Time = Completion Time - Burst Time - Arrival Time
4. Turnaround Time = Completion Time - Arrival Time

GRAPH NEURAL NETWORKS FOR FRAUD DETECTION

Sneha Iyer¹, Meera Joshi²
¹Department of Computer Science, VIT Vellore
²Department of Data Science, Manipal Institute of Technology

Abstract—
This paper presents a novel approach to graph neural networks for fraud detection using state-of-the-art machine learning
techniques. We propose a framework that addresses key challenges in FinTech by leveraging
GNN architectures. Experimental results on benchmark datasets demonstrate
that our method achieves superior performance compared to existing baselines, with an improvement
of 4% in accuracy and 10% reduction in computational overhead.
Our ablation study confirms the contribution of each component.

Keywords— GNN, node classification, transactions, neural networks, fintech, benchmark evaluation

I. INTRODUCTION

The rapid advancement of artificial intelligence has enabled significant breakthroughs in FinTech.
Traditional approaches suffer from limitations including high computational cost, lack of scalability,
and poor generalization to unseen data. Recent works [1,2,3] have attempted to address these challenges
using deep learning, but remain constrained by data availability and model interpretability.

In this paper, we make the following contributions:
(1) We propose a novel GNN-based architecture optimized for FinTech
(2) We introduce a training strategy that reduces overfitting by 31%
(3) We conduct extensive experiments on 5 benchmark datasets
(4) We release our code and pre-trained models for reproducibility

II. RELATED WORK

Several approaches have been proposed for graph neural networks for fraud detection. Smith et al. [1] introduced a CNN-based
method achieving 83% accuracy on standard benchmarks. Lee et al. [2] proposed
an attention mechanism that improves feature extraction but requires 8x more
parameters. Wang et al. [3] applied transfer learning from ImageNet, achieving state-of-the-art
results on domain-specific tasks. Our work differs by addressing computational efficiency without
sacrificing performance.

III. METHODOLOGY

Our proposed framework consists of three main components:

A. Feature Extraction Module
We utilize a modified Vision Transformer
backbone pre-trained on JFT-300M for robust feature
extraction. The backbone is fine-tuned with a learning rate of 0.001.

B. Classification Head
A 2-layer MLP with dropout (p=0.4) is attached
to the backbone output. We use ReLU activations.

C. Training Objective
We optimize using SGD with Momentum with weight decay
1e-05. The loss function is cross-entropy combined with a
regularization term λ=0.1.

IV. EXPERIMENTS

Datasets: We evaluate on 3 public benchmarks.
Implementation: PyTorch 2.0, NVIDIA A100 GPU, batch size 32.

Results: Our method achieves 93.6% accuracy on the primary benchmark,
outperforming baselines by 7%. Inference time is 31ms per sample.

V. CONCLUSION

We presented a novel approach to graph neural networks for fraud detection demonstrating significant improvements over
state-of-the-art methods. Future work will explore multi-modal inputs.

REFERENCES
[1] A. Smith et al., "Deep Learning for FinTech," IEEE CVPR, 2022.
[2] B. Lee et al., "Attention Mechanisms in GNN," NeurIPS, 2023.
[3] C. Wang et al., "Transfer Learning Survey," IEEE TPAMI, 2024.

Abstract—
This paper presents a novel approach to deep learning for medical image segmentation using state-of-the-art machine learning
techniques. We propose a framework that addresses key challenges in Healthcare AI by leveraging
CNN architectures. Experimental results on benchmark datasets demonstrate
that our method achieves superior performance compared to existing baselines, with an improvement
of 3% in accuracy and 18% reduction in computational overhead.
Our ablation study confirms the contribution of each component.

Keywords— CNN, U-Net, MRI, segmentation, neural networks, healthcare ai, benchmark evaluation

I. INTRODUCTION

The rapid advancement of artificial intelligence has enabled significant breakthroughs in Healthcare AI.
Traditional approaches suffer from limitations including high computational cost, lack of scalability,
and poor generalization to unseen data. Recent works [1,2,3] have attempted to address these challenges
using deep learning, but remain constrained by data availability and model interpretability.

In this paper, we make the following contributions:
(1) We propose a novel CNN-based architecture optimized for Healthcare AI
(2) We introduce a training strategy that reduces overfitting by 21%
(3) We conduct extensive experiments on 4 benchmark datasets
(4) We release our code and pre-trained models for reproducibility

II. RELATED WORK

Several approaches have been proposed for deep learning for medical image segmentation. Smith et al. [1] introduced a CNN-based
method achieving 86% accuracy on standard benchmarks. Lee et al. [2] proposed
an attention mechanism that improves feature extraction but requires 3x more
parameters. Wang et al. [3] applied transfer learning from ImageNet, achieving state-of-the-art
results on domain-specific tasks. Our work differs by addressing computational efficiency without
sacrificing performance.

III. METHODOLOGY

Our proposed framework consists of three main components:

A. Feature Extraction Module
We utilize a modified Vision Transformer
backbone pre-trained on JFT-300M for robust feature
extraction. The backbone is fine-tuned with a learning rate of 0.0001.

B. Classification Head
A 4-layer MLP with dropout (p=0.3) is attached
to the backbone output. We use ReLU activations.

C. Training Objective
We optimize using SGD with Momentum with weight decay
1e-05. The loss function is cross-entropy combined with a
regularization term λ=0.1.

IV. EXPERIMENTS

Datasets: We evaluate on 3 public benchmarks.
Implementation: PyTorch 1.12, NVIDIA A100 GPU, batch size 32.

Results: Our method achieves 91.9% accuracy on the primary benchmark,
outperforming baselines by 5%. Inference time is 34ms per sample.

V. CONCLUSION

We presented a novel approach to deep learning for medical image segmentation demonstrating significant improvements over
state-of-the-art methods. Future work will explore zero-shot generalization.

LABORATORY REPORT

Experiment Name: Digital Logic Gate Verification
Subject: Electronics Lab
Student Name: Ravi Bhat
Roll Number: 204562
Batch: C2
Date of Experiment: 3/12/2024
Date of Submission: 3/12/2024

AIM / OBJECTIVE:
To verify and study digital logic gate verification and to understand the relationship between
AND and OR under controlled conditions.

THEORY / BACKGROUND:
Digital Logic Gate Verification is a fundamental concept in Electronics. According to the theoretical framework,
AND is directly proportional to OR
when NOT remains constant. This relationship can be expressed
mathematically and verified through controlled experimentation.

APPARATUS / MATERIALS REQUIRED:
1. Voltmeter
2. Breadboard
3. Resistors (various values)
4. pH meter
5. Rheostat

PROCEDURE:
1. Set up the apparatus as shown in the circuit/experimental diagram.
2. Ensure all connections are secure and the circuit is properly grounded.
3. Gradually vary AND from minimum to maximum range.
4. For each setting, record the corresponding OR.
5. Repeat each reading 4 times to minimize random errors.
6. Tabulate all observations carefully.
7. Plot the graph of AND vs OR.

OBSERVATIONS:

    S.No.     AND             OR             
    ────────────────────────────────────────
    1         8.14          2.56
    2         8.43          7.21
    3         6.19          9.17
    4         6.36          3.7
    5         7.58          6.19
    6         1.71          1.5
    7         7.94          4.13

CALCULATIONS:
Mean value = 4.923
Standard Deviation = 2.516
Percentage Error = 3.61%

GRAPH:
A graph of AND vs OR was plotted.
The graph shows a logarithmic relationship,
confirming the theoretical prediction.

RESULT:
The experiment successfully verified Digital Logic Gate Verification. The experimentally obtained value of
NOT = 6.359 Ω
The theoretical value = 32.612
Percentage error = 2.87%

SOURCES OF ERROR:
1. Instrumental errors due to limited precision of measuring instruments
2. Random errors due to fluctuations in AND
3. Human parallax error during reading
4. Contact resistance in connections (for electrical experiments)

CONCLUSION:
The experiment was performed successfully. The results confirm the theoretical relationship
between AND and OR. The percentage
error of 1.7% is within acceptable limits, indicating accurate
experimental technique.

PRECAUTIONS:
1. All readings were taken after the instrument had stabilized.
2. The experiment was conducted under constant room temperature (27°C).
3. All glassware was cleaned and dried before use.

text:
SOFTWARE ENGINEERING — LECTURE NOTES
Unit 3: Design Patterns
Date: 10/11/2024

============================================================
CHAPTER OVERVIEW
============================================================

This unit covers fundamental concepts of MVC in the context of software engineering.
By the end of this unit, students should be able to:
• Explain the concept of MVC and its significance
• Differentiate between singleton and factory
• Apply theoretical concepts to solve problems
• Analyze real-world scenarios involving MVC

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TOPIC 1: INTRODUCTION TO MVC
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Definition:
A MVC is defined as an algorithm that systematically explores nodes in a graph structure.

Key Properties:
1. Efficiency — must satisfy all MVC requirements
2. Consistency — performance under increasing load
3. Durability — behavior in failure scenarios

Example:
Consider a system with 5 processes/nodes. The MVC operates
as follows:
Step 1: Initialize the MVC with default parameters
Step 2: Apply singleton to determine priority/order
Step 3: Execute the operation while maintaining factory invariants
Step 4: Verify correctness using post-conditions

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TOPIC 2: SINGLETON VS FACTORY
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Comparison Table:
┌─────────────────┬───────────────────────┬───────────────────────┐
│ Parameter        │ singleton             │ factory               │
├─────────────────┼───────────────────────┼───────────────────────┤
│ Complexity      │ O(n log n)            │ O(n²)                 │
│ Space           │ O(n)                  │ O(1)                  │
│ Use Case        │ Large datasets        │ Small datasets        │
│ Stability       │ Stable                │ Not stable            │
└─────────────────┴───────────────────────┴───────────────────────┘

Important Note: singleton is preferred when data is already partially sorted.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TOPIC 3: ALGORITHMS & COMPLEXITY ANALYSIS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Algorithm — Mvc:
```
function MVC(input):
    if input is empty:
        return null
    
    result = initialize()
    for each element in input:
        process(element)
        update(result)
    
    return result
```

Time Complexity: O(n²)
Space Complexity: O(n)
Best Case: O(log n)
Worst Case: O(n)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PRACTICE QUESTIONS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. [2 marks] Define MVC and list its properties.

2. [4 marks] Compare and contrast singleton with factory using suitable examples.

3. [6 marks] Given a scenario with 5 concurrent operations, trace the execution
   of MVC step by step.

4. [8 marks] Design an efficient algorithm using MVC for the following problem:
   [Problem description with constraints and expected output]

5. [Previous Year Question 2022] Explain the trade-offs between singleton and
   factory. When would you choose one over the other? (8 marks)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
IMPORTANT FORMULAE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. Efficiency = (Useful Work Done) / (Total Resources Used)
2. Throughput = (Number of Processes Completed) / (Time Period)  
3. Waiting Time = Completion Time - Burst Time - Arrival Time
4. Turnaround Time = Completion Time - Arrival Time

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
REFERENCES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. Silberschatz, Galvin, Gagne — "Software Engineering", 10th Edition, Wiley
2. NPTEL Video Lectures — Software Engineering by Prof. Rohan Gupta, IIT Delhi
3. GeeksForGeeks — MVC tutorials
4. Previous year question papers — SRM University 2021

parameters. Wang et al. [3] applied transfer learning from ImageNet, achieving state-of-the-art
results on domain-specific tasks. Our work differs by addressing computational efficiency without
sacrificing performance.

III. METHODOLOGY

Our proposed framework consists of three main components:

A. Feature Extraction Module
We utilize a modified EfficientNet-B4
backbone pre-trained on LAION-5B for robust feature
extraction. The backbone is fine-tuned with a learning rate of 0.0005.

B. Classification Head
A 3-layer MLP with dropout (p=0.5) is attached
to the backbone output. We use ReLU activations.

C. Training Objective
We optimize using AdamW with weight decay
0.0001. The loss function is cross-entropy combined with a
regularization term λ=0.1.

IV. EXPERIMENTS

Datasets: We evaluate on 3 public benchmarks.
Implementation: PyTorch 1.12, NVIDIA A100 GPU, batch size 16.

Results: Our method achieves 96.3% accuracy on the primary benchmark,
outperforming baselines by 3%. Inference time is 40ms per sample.

V. CONCLUSION

We presented a novel approach to reinforcement learning for autonomous vehicles demonstrating significant improvements over
state-of-the-art methods. Future work will explore real-time deployment.

REFERENCES
[1] A. Smith et al., "Deep Learning for Robotics," IEEE CVPR, 2020.
[2] B. Lee et al., "Attention Mechanisms in reward," NeurIPS, 2021.
[3] C. Wang et al., "Transfer Learning Survey," IEEE TPAMI, 2022.

DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS — LECTURE NOTES
Unit 4: SQL and Transactions
Date: 23/2/2024

============================================================
CHAPTER OVERVIEW
============================================================

This unit covers fundamental concepts of SQL in the context of database management systems.
By the end of this unit, students should be able to:
• Explain the concept of SQL and its significance
• Differentiate between ACID and transaction
• Apply theoretical concepts to solve problems
• Analyze real-world scenarios involving SQL

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TOPIC 1: INTRODUCTION TO SQL
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Definition:
A SQL is defined as an algorithm that systematically explores nodes in a graph structure.

Key Properties:
1. Efficiency — must satisfy all SQL requirements
2. Scalability — performance under increasing load
3. Isolation — behavior in failure scenarios

Example:
Consider a system with 7 processes/nodes. The SQL operates
as follows:
Step 1: Initialize the SQL with default parameters
Step 2: Apply ACID to determine priority/order
Step 3: Execute the operation while maintaining transaction invariants
Step 4: Verify correctness using post-conditions

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TOPIC 2: ACID VS TRANSACTION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Comparison Table:
┌─────────────────┬───────────────────────┬───────────────────────┐
│ Parameter        │ ACID                  │ transaction           │
├─────────────────┼───────────────────────┼───────────────────────┤
│ Complexity      │ O(n log n)            │ O(n²)                 │
│ Space           │ O(n)                  │ O(1)                  │
│ Use Case        │ Large datasets        │ Small datasets        │
│ Stability       │ Stable                │ Not stable            │
└─────────────────┴───────────────────────┴───────────────────────┘

Important Note: ACID is preferred when memory is not a constraint and speed is critical.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TOPIC 3: ALGORITHMS & COMPLEXITY ANALYSIS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Algorithm — Sql:
```
function SQL(input):
    if input is empty:
        return null
    
    result = initialize()
    for each element in input:
        process(element)
        update(result)
    
    return result
```

Time Complexity: O(n)
Space Complexity: O(n)
Best Case: O(log n)
Worst Case: O(n)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PRACTICE QUESTIONS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. [2 marks] Define SQL and list its properties.

2. [4 marks] Compare and contrast ACID with transaction using suitable examples.

3. [6 marks] Given a scenario with 4 concurrent operations, trace the execution
   of SQL step by step.

4. [8 marks] Design an efficient algorithm using SQL for the following problem:
   [Problem description with constraints and expected output]

5. [Previous Year Question 2022] Explain the trade-offs between ACID and
   transaction. When would you choose one over the other? (8 marks)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
IMPORTANT FORMULAE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. Efficiency = (Useful Work Done) / (Total Resources Used)
2. Throughput = (Number of Processes Completed) / (Time Period)  
3. Waiting Time = Completion Time - Burst Time - Arrival Time
4. Turnaround Time = Completion Time - Arrival Time

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
REFERENCES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. Silberschatz, Galvin, Gagne — "Database Management Systems", 10th Edition, Wiley
2. NPTEL Video Lectures — Database Management Systems by Prof. Meera Joshi, IIT Bombay
3. GeeksForGeeks — SQL tutorials
4. Previous year question papers — IIT Delhi 2020

text:
GRAPH NEURAL NETWORKS FOR FRAUD DETECTION

Meera Joshi¹, Vikram Nair²
¹Department of Computer Science, IIT Delhi
²Department of Data Science, NIT Trichy

Abstract—
This paper presents a novel approach to graph neural networks for fraud detection using state-of-the-art machine learning
techniques. We propose a framework that addresses key challenges in FinTech by leveraging
GNN architectures. Experimental results on benchmark datasets demonstrate
that our method achieves superior performance compared to existing baselines, with an improvement
of 15% in accuracy and 31% reduction in computational overhead.
Our ablation study confirms the contribution of each component.

Keywords— GNN, node classification, transactions, neural networks, fintech, benchmark evaluation

I. INTRODUCTION

The rapid advancement of artificial intelligence has enabled significant breakthroughs in FinTech.
Traditional approaches suffer from limitations including high computational cost, lack of scalability,
and poor generalization to unseen data. Recent works [1,2,3] have attempted to address these challenges
using deep learning, but remain constrained by data availability and model interpretability.

In this paper, we make the following contributions:
(1) We propose a novel GNN-based architecture optimized for FinTech
(2) We introduce a training strategy that reduces overfitting by 24%
(3) We conduct extensive experiments on 3 benchmark datasets
(4) We release our code and pre-trained models for reproducibility

II. RELATED WORK

Several approaches have been proposed for graph neural networks for fraud detection. Smith et al. [1] introduced a CNN-based
method achieving 78% accuracy on standard benchmarks. Lee et al. [2] proposed
an attention mechanism that improves feature extraction but requires 5x more
parameters. Wang et al. [3] applied transfer learning from ImageNet, achieving state-of-the-art
results on domain-specific tasks. Our work differs by addressing computational efficiency without
sacrificing performance.

III. METHODOLOGY

Our proposed framework consists of three main components:

A. Feature Extraction Module
We utilize a modified EfficientNet-B4
backbone pre-trained on ImageNet for robust feature
extraction. The backbone is fine-tuned with a learning rate of 0.001.

B. Classification Head
A 2-layer MLP with dropout (p=0.4) is attached
to the backbone output. We use GELU activations.

C. Training Objective
We optimize using AdaFactor with weight decay
0.0001. The loss function is cross-entropy combined with a
regularization term λ=0.1.

IV. EXPERIMENTS

Datasets: We evaluate on 3 public benchmarks.
Implementation: PyTorch 2.0, NVIDIA A100 GPU, batch size 16.

Results: Our method achieves 87.3% accuracy on the primary benchmark,
outperforming baselines by 5%. Inference time is 40ms per sample.

V. CONCLUSION

We presented a novel approach to graph neural networks for fraud detection demonstrating significant improvements over
state-of-the-art methods. Future work will explore zero-shot generalization.

REFERENCES
[1] A. Smith et al., "Deep Learning for FinTech," IEEE CVPR, 2019.
[2] B. Lee et al., "Attention Mechanisms in GNN," NeurIPS, 2020.
[3] C. Wang et al., "Transfer Learning Survey," IEEE TPAMI, 2021.

SOFTWARE ENGINEERING — LECTURE NOTES
Unit 3: Design Patterns
Date: 19/1/2024

============================================================
CHAPTER OVERVIEW
============================================================

This unit covers fundamental concepts of MVC in the context of software engineering.
By the end of this unit, students should be able to:
• Explain the concept of MVC and its significance
• Differentiate between singleton and factory
• Apply theoretical concepts to solve problems
• Analyze real-world scenarios involving MVC

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TOPIC 1: INTRODUCTION TO MVC
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Definition:
A MVC is defined as an algorithm that systematically explores nodes in a graph structure.

Key Properties:
1. Correctness — must satisfy all MVC requirements
2. Scalability — performance under increasing load
3. Isolation — behavior in failure scenarios

Example:
Consider a system with 6 processes/nodes. The MVC operates
as follows:
Step 1: Initialize the MVC with default parameters
Step 2: Apply singleton to determine priority/order
Step 3: Execute the operation while maintaining factory invariants
Step 4: Verify correctness using post-conditions

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TOPIC 2: SINGLETON VS FACTORY
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Comparison Table:
┌─────────────────┬───────────────────────┬───────────────────────┐
│ Parameter        │ singleton             │ factory               │
├─────────────────┼───────────────────────┼───────────────────────┤
│ Complexity      │ O(n log n)            │ O(n²)                 │
│ Space           │ O(n)                  │ O(1)                  │
│ Use Case        │ Large datasets        │ Small datasets        │
│ Stability       │ Stable                │ Not stable            │
└─────────────────┴───────────────────────┴───────────────────────┘

Important Note: singleton is preferred when memory is not a constraint and speed is critical.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TOPIC 3: ALGORITHMS & COMPLEXITY ANALYSIS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Algorithm — Mvc:
```
function MVC(input):
    if input is empty:
        return null
    
    result = initialize()
    for each element in input:
        process(element)
        update(result)
    
    return result
```

Time Complexity: O(n)
Space Complexity: O(n²)
Best Case: O(1)
Worst Case: O(n)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PRACTICE QUESTIONS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. [2 marks] Define MVC and list its properties.

2. [4 marks] Compare and contrast singleton with factory using suitable examples.

3. [6 marks] Given a scenario with 3 concurrent operations, trace the execution
   of MVC step by step.

4. [8 marks] Design an efficient algorithm using MVC for the following problem:
   [Problem description with constraints and expected output]

5. [Previous Year Question 2022] Explain the trade-offs between singleton and
   factory. When would you choose one over the other? (8 marks)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
IMPORTANT FORMULAE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. Efficiency = (Useful Work Done) / (Total Resources Used)
2. Throughput = (Number of Processes Completed) / (Time Period)  
3. Waiting Time = Completion Time - Burst Time - Arrival Time
4. Turnaround Time = Completion Time - Arrival Time

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
REFERENCES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. Silberschatz, Galvin, Gagne — "Software Engineering", 10th Edition, Wiley
2. NPTEL Video Lectures — Software Engineering by Prof. Neha Agarwal, IIT Madras
3. GeeksForGeeks — MVC tutorials
4. Previous year question papers — IIT Delhi 2021

LABORATORY REPORT

Experiment Name: Titration of Acids and Bases
Subject: Chemistry Lab
Student Name: Rohan Gupta
Roll Number: 207959
Batch: C1
Date of Experiment: 1/10/2024
Date of Submission: 1/10/2024

AIM / OBJECTIVE:
To verify and study titration of acids and bases and to understand the relationship between
molarity and equivalence point under controlled conditions.

THEORY / BACKGROUND:
Titration of Acids and Bases is a fundamental concept in Chemistry. According to the theoretical framework,
molarity is directly proportional to equivalence point
when pH remains constant. This relationship can be expressed
mathematically and verified through controlled experimentation.

APPARATUS / MATERIALS REQUIRED:
1. Oscilloscope
2. Breadboard
3. Resistors (various values)
4. Multimeter
5. Conical flask

PROCEDURE:
1. Set up the apparatus as shown in the circuit/experimental diagram.
2. Ensure all connections are secure and the circuit is properly grounded.
3. Gradually vary molarity from minimum to maximum range.
4. For each setting, record the corresponding equivalence point.
5. Repeat each reading 3 times to minimize random errors.
6. Tabulate all observations carefully.
7. Plot the graph of molarity vs equivalence point.

OBSERVATIONS:

    S.No.     molarity        equivalence point
    ────────────────────────────────────────
    1         8.62          3.13
    2         5.52          4.7
    3         5.92          4.3
    4         9.8          6.11
    5         7.34          2.02
    6         5.33          7.21
    7         2.28          7.58
    8         9.85          9.85

CALCULATIONS:
Mean value = 5.613
Standard Deviation = 2.404
Percentage Error = 3.02%

GRAPH:
A graph of molarity vs equivalence point was plotted.
The graph shows a linear relationship,
confirming the theoretical prediction.

RESULT:
The experiment successfully verified Titration of Acids and Bases. The experimentally obtained value of
pH = 68.723 N/m
The theoretical value = 61.691
Percentage error = 4.53%

SOURCES OF ERROR:
1. Instrumental errors due to limited precision of measuring instruments
2. Random errors due to fluctuations in molarity
3. Human parallax error during reading
4. Contact resistance in connections (for electrical experiments)

CONCLUSION:
The experiment was performed successfully. The results confirm the theoretical relationship
between molarity and equivalence point. The percentage
error of 2.72% is within acceptable limits, indicating accurate
experimental technique.

PRECAUTIONS:
1. All readings were taken after the instrument had stabilized.
2. The experiment was conducted under constant room temperature (22°C).
3. All glassware was cleaned and dried before use.

text:
TRANSFORMER ARCHITECTURE FOR CODE GENERATION

Priya Patel¹, Sneha Iyer²
¹Department of Computer Science, Mumbai University
²Department of Data Science, IIIT Hyderabad

Abstract—
This paper presents a novel approach to transformer architecture for code generation using state-of-the-art machine learning
techniques. We propose a framework that addresses key challenges in NLP by leveraging
GPT architectures. Experimental results on benchmark datasets demonstrate
that our method achieves superior performance compared to existing baselines, with an improvement
of 3% in accuracy and 21% reduction in computational overhead.
Our ablation study confirms the contribution of each component.

Keywords— GPT, code synthesis, program generation, neural networks, nlp, benchmark evaluation

I. INTRODUCTION

The rapid advancement of artificial intelligence has enabled significant breakthroughs in NLP.
Traditional approaches suffer from limitations including high computational cost, lack of scalability,
and poor generalization to unseen data. Recent works [1,2,3] have attempted to address these challenges
using deep learning, but remain constrained by data availability and model interpretability.

In this paper, we make the following contributions:
(1) We propose a novel GPT-based architecture optimized for NLP
(2) We introduce a training strategy that reduces overfitting by 25%
(3) We conduct extensive experiments on 4 benchmark datasets
(4) We release our code and pre-trained models for reproducibility

II. RELATED WORK

Several approaches have been proposed for transformer architecture for code generation. Smith et al. [1] introduced a CNN-based
method achieving 81% accuracy on standard benchmarks. Lee et al. [2] proposed
an attention mechanism that improves feature extraction but requires 7x more
parameters. Wang et al. [3] applied transfer learning from ImageNet, achieving state-of-the-art
results on domain-specific tasks. Our work differs by addressing computational efficiency without
sacrificing performance.

III. METHODOLOGY

Our proposed framework consists of three main components:

A. Feature Extraction Module
We utilize a modified MobileNetV3
backbone pre-trained on JFT-300M for robust feature
extraction. The backbone is fine-tuned with a learning rate of 0.001.

B. Classification Head
A 4-layer MLP with dropout (p=0.3) is attached
to the backbone output. We use GELU activations.

C. Training Objective
We optimize using AdamW with weight decay
0.0001. The loss function is cross-entropy combined with a
regularization term λ=0.1.

IV. EXPERIMENTS

Datasets: We evaluate on 2 public benchmarks.
Implementation: PyTorch 1.12, NVIDIA A100 GPU, batch size 32.

Results: Our method achieves 89.2% accuracy on the primary benchmark,
outperforming baselines by 2%. Inference time is 37ms per sample.

V. CONCLUSION

We presented a novel approach to transformer architecture for code generation demonstrating significant improvements over
state-of-the-art methods. Future work will explore zero-shot generalization.

REFERENCES
[1] A. Smith et al., "Deep Learning for NLP," IEEE CVPR, 2019.
[2] B. Lee et al., "Attention Mechanisms in GPT," NeurIPS, 2020.
[3] C. Wang et al., "Transfer Learning Survey," IEEE TPAMI, 2021.
label: research_paper
